palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's chatting is fully outsourced (§8) and there is no documented persona-briefing process internally. When Palm builds in-house or AI chatting, voice consistency will be critical — especially for AI personas (§3) where breaking character destroys the illusion.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add a 'chatter voice card' field to the Creators table in Airtable (and AI Account Profile for AI personas). Populate: age, typing quirks, signature emojis, pet names, niche. Feed this card to any chatter (human or AI) at session start. Especially high priority for AI persona chatting.

consensus_risers.title: Proactively disclose account issues to creators — hiding problems destroys trust faster than the problem itself

variants.claim: Agencies that hide negative account developments lose model trust catastrophically when the model discovers the issue independently; proactive disclosure, even of bad news, strengthens the relationship.
variants.what_they_do: Their agency policy is to give updates on account performance whether good or bad. If something goes wrong (traffic drop, platform issue, content underperformance), they inform the model directly rather than waiting or papering over it. This is presented as a cultural value established from onboarding onwards.

variants.claim: Being fully transparent with models about where their money goes, admitting when you're failing and why, and framing everything around their long-term income sustainability earns far more loyalty than over-promising.
variants.what_they_do: He tells creators explicitly where agency fees go and how ad spend is allocated. When metrics underperform, he proactively explains why rather than going silent. When a creator wants to leave, he releases them without conflict and often admits his own faults. He reports that 8 out of 10 creators who leave return. He pitches creators with numbers and technical detail — no yachts, no content houses, no collab promises.

variants.claim: Most creator-agency relationship problems stem from unclear communication about what the agency does and why, not from the tactics themselves.
variants.what_they_do: Dylan's top lesson is that agencies fail creators by not explaining the reasoning behind decisions. When a creator complains or pushes back, the wrong response is 'we just do this because it works.' The right response is to genuinely understand what's bothering them — is it the content type? The social media time demand? The lack of transparency? He builds in active listening and clear explanation as a core operational habit, not an ad-hoc fix.

sources.quote: you don't want to just make it seem like everything's going perfectly and them to figure out something's wrong on their own because that's going to be just relationship destroying
being fully transparent is very important to me. If I'm failing at something, I will tell them I'm failing at this thing. Now I'll tell them why I'm failing at it
if you want to leave and go somewhere else, I'm not going to fight you... eight out of 10 times they'll revisit
a lot of issues that are going to arise are going to be from you not communicating clearly with your client on like what you do and just setting expectations framing stuff the correct way

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented creator communications policy around bad news or account issues (§6 thin, §9 no retention strategy). The weekly invoice cadence provides a financial signal but there is no SOP for flagging non-financial account problems to creators.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add a 'bad news protocol' to Palm's creator comms SOP: define what events trigger a proactive creator notification (e.g., >20% subscriber drop, platform flag, traffic source failure) and set a response-time SLA (e.g., notify within 24 hours). Document this protocol and mention it during onboarding so creators know to expect it. Supports §9 retention.

consensus_risers.topic: Chat performance tracking within delivery operating system
consensus_risers.title: Track chat performance weekly as a core delivery KPI — don't discover underperformance after revenue drops

variants.claim: Chatters underperforming is typically caught too late because there is no proactive weekly tracking system — by the time revenue drops, the damage is already done.
variants.what_they_do: Dylan embeds chat performance as one of the seven required pieces of a delivery operating system: content posted, PPV performance, rebuilds, expired fans, chat performance, creator compliance, creator complaints. The chatter manager owns chat revenue and quality as their primary number. Weekly scorecard review catches a declining chatter before the month closes. He contrasts reactive delivery (everyone panics after revenue drops) with proactive delivery (problem spotted before it becomes painful via data and tracking).

variants.claim: Adding basic per-chatter PPV-send-count tracking immediately identifies underperforming chatters who were previously invisible, enabling targeted fixes that can 10× an individual chatter's weekly revenue.
variants.what_they_do: The agency added a simple KPI dashboard tracking PPVs sent per chatter per week. This immediately revealed one chatter sending 60–70 PPVs/week while others sent 150+; confronting that chatter with the data and coaching him moved his weekly revenue from ~$300 to ~$4,000. Previously the agency ran entirely on 'feelings' — no numbers, no comparison. They also track the PPV open/buy rate per individual PPV piece to spot which content is underperforming and fix the drop specifically rather than chasing new traffic.

A chatter misses standards and nothing happens. That's the culture.

I figured out was guy one guy was not making the money that it was supposedly to be making
the other chatter was sending like 150 pays a week for example and then the guy was having like 60 70 pay perviews a week
it's not selling the way that it should be. It's a drop. Just fixing that drop

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm currently outsources DM monetization to an external chat team (§8) and has no internal chat performance SOP. The whale-hunting module and Fan Tracker CRM (§9) capture some fan-side data, but there is no weekly chat performance scorecard tied to the delivery review cadence. This is build-toward intelligence for both the in-house chat team and AI chatting roadmap projects (§8).
palm_comparison.recommendation: As Palm moves toward in-house chatting, define the chat performance KPIs that the future chatter manager will own: PPV conversion rate, average revenue per fan per week, response time, welcome message performance, and reactivation rate on going-cold fans. Build these into the weekly scorecard from day one of in-house operations rather than retrofitting later. In the interim, request these metrics from the external chat team weekly and add them to Palm's existing Fan Tracker / whale-hunting reporting (§9) to start building baseline data now.

consensus_risers.topic: Employee onboarding and task delegation framework
consensus_risers.title: Five-step delegation system: do it yourself → document → idiot-proof → work alongside → KPI-gate

variants.claim: Agencies fail to delegate because they skip directly to handing off tasks; the correct sequence is to personally do the task on Loom first, then optimize, then simplify to yes/no checklist questions, then shadow the employee daily until the KPI is hit consistently.
variants.what_they_do: Step 1: Owner does the task themselves while recording a Loom with live commentary explaining every decision. Step 2: Identify friction points discovered while doing it. Step 3: Reduce all subjective criteria to yes/no binary questions (e.g. 'Does the background look like she actually lives there? Yes/No'; 'Did you see this location in the last 10 reels? Yes/No'). Step 4: Work alongside the employee with daily 15–30 min screen-share standups until they hit the KPI independently. Step 5: Define one clear KPI for the role (e.g. 'minimum 100 free Reddit subs per day') — if they hit it, method doesn't matter; if they miss it, run a three-question diagnostic: Did you follow the SOP? Did you ask for help? If both no → strike system (3 strikes = termination or 30% salary cut). For new hires, drip SOPs week-by-week rather than dumping all documentation at once. Offer a bonus for employees who return a document of every question the SOP failed to answer.

variants.claim: Founders scale out of the business by delegating outcomes and decision-making (an ownership ladder), not by dumping tasks, which only deepens dependency.
variants.what_they_do: Audits dollar-value-per-hour (only hires when a low-DVPH task blocks a high one), develops people up a level 1-5 accountability dial from doer to owner, and installs a decision filter so nobody escalates a problem without bullets + 2-3 solutions + a recommendation; uses the '30-day disappear test' to find the ceiling.

sources.quote: we want to really like do the task ourselves and document it so for example in the agency I always just start a loom video
the minimum it's basically the 100 free subs. With the system 100 free subs
did you follow the SOPs and the sheets and the instructions word by word? If the answer is yes, then it's not his fault
you can break out most task into very simple questions like even chatting
delegation actually works is if you delegate outcomes plus decision-making instead of delegating tasks
never bring me a problem without what happened... 2-3 solutions, your recommendation

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented delegation or employee-onboarding framework (§10, THIN). The content pipeline has an 8-step SOP (§1) but no structured handoff process for training editors or managers to own it. This framework applies directly to onboarding editors, the Reddit/posting VA (Amin), and future in-house chatters.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Adopt this five-step framework as Palm's standard delegation SOP. Apply it immediately to: (1) the editor workflow in §1 — record Loom walkthroughs of the day-counter queue, (2) any Reddit/traffic VAs Palm adds (§5 gap), (3) the first in-house chatter hire. Add a 'role KPI' field to Clerk/Airtable role definitions. Build a simple strike-tracking table in Airtable for team accountability.

consensus_risers.title: Promote staff up the five levels of accountability to remove yourself as bottleneck

variants.claim: Scale past a plateau by training department heads to own problems end-to-end rather than escalating every decision to the owner.
variants.what_they_do: Five accountability levels: (1) wait to be told, (2) bring the problem, (3) bring problem + 1-2 solutions, (4) solve it and report, (5) solve it AND build an SOP so it never recurs. Most teams sit at 1-2, making the owner the bottleneck. Push C-suite heads to level 4-5; example: an acquisition manager independently bought aged warmed IG accounts and hired another VA to lift outreach capacity, then just reported it. Hold Mon-Fri morning team calls with only the department heads.

variants.claim: Hires should be added on a revenue-gated ladder — owner does everything to ~5k, VAs/chatters at 5-20k, managers at 20k+, ops director at 50-100k+.
variants.what_they_do: 0-5k owner solo; 5-20k first VA/chatter (after chatting yourself 2 weeks); 20-40k chat manager first then content/account managers; 50-100k+ ops director to execute proven systems so the owner exits day-to-day.

variants.claim: Free the owner by splitting the agency into core departments, each run by a manager the owner reports through, instead of doing everything personally.
variants.what_they_do: Typically three departments — client acquisition, marketing, chatting — each with a manager (e.g. chatter manager, marketing director, acquisition manager). The owner checks in with managers (not every IC) once each morning; managers run the ICs (outreach VAs, editors, chatters). Beginners can start with 3-6 chatters + one chatter manager. Trying to do it all solo makes scaling to $1M/mo 'physically impossible.'

sources.quote: there are five main levels of accountability... the first level being they just simply wait for the command on what to do.
what's different between level five and level four is they not only find the problem and solve it, but they also will build infrastructure to ensure that this problem never happens again.
making sure that you guys do morning team calls Monday to Friday.
you need to hire managers
you want to have three four main departments... Client acquisition, marketing, and chatting... have a manager in client acquisition, a manager in marketing, and a manager in chatting.
once in the morning, you check in with all of the managers.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no hiring scorecards, accountability framework, or management cadence documented (§10 GAP).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Adopt the five-level accountability ladder as a management standard, push leads to level 4-5 with SOP-building expectations, and run Mon-Fri morning leadership calls.

consensus_risers.title: Weekly async Loom chat reviews focused on new subscribers + high-ticket purchases

variants.claim: Regular async video reviews (via Loom) of specific conversation types — new subscribers, purchases, high-ticket sales — plus chatter-submitted chats drive measurable skill improvement.
variants.what_they_do: Manager opens the 'new subscribers' tab and reviews those conversations each week. Also reviews purchase logs and high-ticket sales. Asks each chatter to submit specific chats for review. Records Loom feedback videos. Per-review focus stays on one improvement point. Maintains 'scientist mode' (critical, constructive) not 'dictator mode' (anger-driven). Each chatter gets one weekly improvement point plus actionable steps.

variants.claim: Requiring chatters to pre-prepare their own good/bad chat examples before a weekly standup forces self-accountability, reduces manager review time by ~20 hrs/week, and improves chatter quality proactively.
variants.what_they_do: Before each weekly standup call, every chatter (or supervisor representing the chatter team) must prepare: all problems/questions from the week, five chats where they performed well, and five chats where they performed poorly. The explicit rule: 'If I find bad chats you didn't show me, I'll be mad — better bring them now while I'm in a good mood.' This flips the review burden to the chatter. The agency owner then only needs ~5 minutes to scan before the call. At scale, one supervisor handles all chatters and consolidates the prep; the owner only runs one standup with the supervisor. The agency estimated this system recovers ~20 hours/week of manager time.

sources.quote: We open up the new subscribers tab and read those conversations. We look at purchases. We look for high ticket sales. And we ask chatters every week to give us specific chats to review
Every week we analyze the one point of improvement for each chatter
prepare all the problems you have, the questions that you want to answer, five chats where you think it went amazing, and five chats where you think you did shit
if I find chats that are bad and you didn't show them to me in a call, I'm going to be mad
I think it was like 20 hours per week just into reviewing everything

palm_comparison.vs_us: No internal review cadence at Palm (§8, outsourced). Partially relevant to the AI chatting project as well. Build-toward.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Design a weekly review ritual into the in-house chat SOP from day one: manager records a ≤5-min Loom per chatter, logs the single focus point and action steps in Airtable (add a 'Chatter Reviews' table under the chat team's workspace).

consensus_risers.topic: OF vault folder structure for chatter efficiency
consensus_risers.title: Organise OF vault into named script folders so chatters can locate PPV assets instantly

variants.claim: Structuring the OF content vault into named folders (e.g. 'Script 1', 'New Subscriber', 'PPV', 'SFW Feed') tied to specific chat scripts reduces chatter errors and speeds up PPV delivery during live conversations.
variants.what_they_do: Inside the OF vault the agency creates dedicated folders for: (1) each reusable sexting script (Script 1, Script 2, etc.) containing all media needed for that session; (2) a 'New Subscriber' folder with content specifically used in the new-sub welcome sequence; (3) a general 'PPV' folder; (4) optionally an 'SFW' folder for feed reposts. When a chatter replays a script with a new subscriber they navigate to the matching script folder to pull the correct assets in order.

variants.claim: A disorganized vault and generic hotkeys are the single largest hidden tax on chatter productivity; restructuring vault with categories and building custom per-creator hotkeys raised reply rates 60% in two weeks.
variants.what_they_do: Vault restructured within week 1: every content piece labeled by category (boy/girl, tier list, JOI, rapport building), exact price per video, and a defined script order (first PPV → second PPV → optional upsell → conditional branches). Chatters open CreatorHero, see the script order, copy-paste, send — 50-second reply time, no scrolling. Hotkeys are custom per creator: matched to the creator's speaking style, age, aesthetic, and specific vault content. Separate hotkeys for small talk, transitions, objection handling. The 18-year-old blonde's hotkeys use youth lingo; the milf's don't. Result: chatters spend 80% less time searching, 80% more time selling.

sources.quote: if you have big sexting scripts...you'll create folders in the vault called like script one script two Etc which is where all the content relating to that script will go
the easier you make it for them the less chance of mistakes there will be
we label everything. categories, scripts, boy, girl, tier list, jerk off instructions, rapport building, every single pricing for the video, and the script order
we build custom hotkeys per creator, per script, and they are specific to the way she speaks, the way she looks
our reply rate went up by like 60% in the first two weeks just from this

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's OF vault/PPV content system is THIN (§2) and chatting is currently outsourced (§8). There is no documented vault folder convention or script-to-asset mapping. This is directly relevant to Palm's roadmap item of building an in-house chat team and AI chatting system.
palm_comparison.recommendation: As build-toward for the in-house chat team: design the OF vault structure around scripts rather than raw chronology. Create a folder schema (New Subscriber, Script_01…Script_N, PPV_unlabelled, SFW_Feed) before handing off to chatters. Document which assets belong to which script in Airtable so the AI chatting system can programmatically select the right media during a conversation.

consensus_risers.topic: PPV price escalation ladders by fan tier
consensus_risers.title: Tiered PPV price escalation: 50% steps for low spenders, 70% steps for high spenders; break $200 cap via tip + multi-unlock

variants.claim: PPV prices should escalate by a set percentage each step within a loop, calibrated to the fan's spending tier, and the $200 OnlyFans cap is not a real ceiling — it can be bypassed via tips or multi-PPV unlocks.
variants.what_they_do: Low spenders (max purchase <$50): increase price ~50% per step. Example ladder: $35 → $55 → $85 → $125. High spenders (max purchase >$120): increase ~70% per step. Example ladder: $50 → $85 → $150 → $255. To exceed the $200 OF cap: (a) request a tip first, then send the PPV, or (b) split into multiple PPVs (e.g. 4×$200 = $800 total) framed as 'unlock these now and I'll send the main video while I get it from my archive.' Always frame multi-unlocks as a favor/bonus. If a subscriber accepts a dropped price too easily, immediately add more PPVs to the bundle to recover revenue ('I had an idea — because it's you, I want to throw in some extra stuff').

variants.claim: Setting price per fan from their purchase history, backing off after a no and pushing higher for proven whales, beats one fixed price for all.
variants.what_they_do: Luca Pritchard uses dynamic pricing read from each subscriber's unlock history via tools like Infloww: if a fan stalled at $65 he offers 17/27/47/59 next time, but if a fan will pay $150-$200 for what others pay $20 for he sells at that willingness, and he keeps explicit content premium-priced so perceived value stays high.

sources.quote: for low spenders, which I would define as someone with a maximum purchase of $50, we can increase the price each time by about 50%
for higher spenders, subscribers with a maximum purchase of over $120, we can try and increase the price by about 70% each time
you can't send a pay-per-view over $200. Sure, you can't, but you can still get around that in other ways
you pick a different selling strategy for every single subscriber
the low price might make him not want to buy instead of buy... keep the content premium priced

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented PPV pricing model or escalation framework (§7 GAP — explicitly called out as the #1 creator concern). This is directly applicable intelligence for building Palm's pricing playbook.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Document these two ladders (50% / 70% escalation by tier) as Palm's default PPV pricing framework. Add the $200-bypass mechanics (tip-first, multi-unlock bundle) to chatter SOPs. Critically, also adopt the 'add more content rather than discount' rule for objection handling — this protects price integrity over time and should be a standing chatter policy.

consensus_risers.title: Vertical vs. horizontal scale: choose model before building ops

variants.claim: OFM agencies must consciously choose between vertical scale (few creators, salary model, high margin, high risk) and horizontal scale (many creators, rev-share, lower margin, lower risk) because the ops, team size, and marketing obligations are fundamentally different.
variants.what_they_do: Hunter maps two distinct agency architectures: vertical = 1–few salary creators, agency keeps ~90% of revenue, agency owns all marketing; horizontal = many rev-share creators who self-market with agency-provided SOPs, agency takes ~30% cut plus upsell fees. He recommends starting horizontal, then using that cash flow to fund a vertical page for higher margins.

variants.claim: Agencies should consciously choose between scaling horizontally (more creators, same system) or vertically (fewer creators, maximize each) rather than defaulting to signing as many models as possible, because more models often means more headache without proportionally more profit.
variants.what_they_do: Renato lays out two explicit scaling modes: (1) Horizontal — sign 10+ models, apply the same playbook to each, multiply net profit proportionally; (2) Vertical — take 1–4 models and push each to maximum revenue. He warns that multi-model agencies frequently have low per-person net profit when the team is split 3–4 ways. He recommends operators choose a mode deliberately, then structure hiring, content systems, and deal types accordingly. His own preference trends toward fewer models with salary deals and owned traffic.

sources.quote: There are two ways that you can scale an OFM company. Okay? You can either vertically scale.
I would take the income that I'm making from that operation and then start injecting it into vertical scale models on a vertical scale page and have the best of both worlds.
you can scale horizontally. So, you can have He has four models. You can sign 10 more models, do the same for them, and you'll be happy.
Most people that have many models, that is just a headache. They're gambling, and their agencies rarely make that much profit.
you can make 500k in gross revenue, but how much is each individual person taking home? 30k? 40k? Well, then it's better to be Alexander.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm currently operates as a hybrid leaning vertical — real creators with a revenue-split model, Palm owning social/content and outsourcing chat. Palm's commission structure is per-creator at onboarding (§7) but no documented strategic rationale for vertical vs. horizontal positioning. No explicit decision framework exists.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Document Palm's explicit strategic choice between vertical and horizontal, or define a hybrid model. If Palm wants to scale to many creators, formalize the horizontal SOP-provision model (optimize what creators already have). If Palm stays vertical with fewer high-value creators, document the salary-vs-split decision criteria. At minimum, use this framework in founder strategy sessions.

consensus_risers.topic: Client wins database as retention and sales ammo
consensus_risers.title: Maintain a running 'wins database' — screenshots, metrics, clips — per client for retention and resigning

variants.claim: Systematically capturing and storing client wins (lead screenshots, view counts, clips) in a tagged database gives the CSM live ammunition to counter 'are you doing anything for me?' objections, improves retention at renewal, and provides case study material for new sales.
variants.what_they_do: Notion 'wins' database with fields: win type (leads/views/revenue/video performance), client relation, screenshot or media URL, date. Anyone on the team can add a win. KPI: minimum X wins per client per month set as a CSM metric. Wins roll up to client OS so the CSM can open a client record and instantly see a count and list of wins. Used on retention calls, renewals, and sales calls. Goal: ~20+ wins/month company-wide as a tracked number on the client success dashboard.

variants.claim: Agencies should run a standing mechanism that captures every creator win, screenshot, and testimonial as it happens, since proof fuels both acquisition and trust.
variants.what_they_do: Routes every positive creator message, analytics screenshot, and voice note into a Drive/table immediately, so each win can later be turned into an ad or case study; recommends installing this even before running ads.

sources.quote: we want to create a culture at our company that we collect wins, we celebrate wins, and we stack wins. We have almost a thousand wins of just screenshots
I would try to get like at least a couple of wins a month for each client and make that like your KPI internally from like a client success perspective
when you're on a call with a client and they're like, 'Oh, I'm just not really seeing anything.' It's like, 'Dude, like we've gotten you 10 leads.'
start installing the systems to capture proof like immediately
you need to like start having a system within your agency that captures proof

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has whale-hunting and Fan Tracker tools (§9) for fan-level data, but no documented system for capturing and storing creator wins (viral posts, subscriber milestones, revenue records) as a retention tool. The §9 gap also notes no written retention strategy. A wins database is directly applicable: Palm's editors and social team observe creator wins daily and could log them systematically.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add a 'Creator Wins' table in Airtable (Content Ops or HQ base) with fields: creator, win type (milestone sub count, viral reel, PPV revenue record, press mention), media URL, date. Make logging a win a required step for editors/social team when they observe a notable metric. Josh or a creator success role reviews and shares wins back to creators weekly — this doubles as creator retention messaging and provides case study content for acquisition.

consensus_risers.topic: Cloud phone farm for IG account amplification
consensus_risers.title: Operate 200–300 cloud-based IG accounts to funnel traffic to creator main pages

variants.claim: Running hundreds of warmed-up Instagram accounts via cloud phones on automated schedules generates 50–200 additional subs per day per creator while insulating the main account from bans.
variants.what_they_do: Agency spins up 200–300 Instagram accounts on cloud-hosted real phones. Automations handle: feed scrolling, liking, Reel posting, phone-number verification — all to mimic organic human behavior and avoid suspensions. None of these satellite accounts carry the OF link in bio; they funnel only to the creator's main IG page (or to 5 intermediate accounts). The OF link lives only on the main page. Suspension rate drops to ~1-in-20 vs. higher rates when the OF link is in satellite bios. If main account is banned, warmed satellite accounts are handed to the creator to restart on.

variants.claim: Multiply reach by creating multiple secondary social accounts and posting across many platforms, then funnel all of it to OnlyFans.
variants.what_they_do: Beyond the creator's main IG, create 2nd/3rd/4th IG accounts plus Reddit, Snapchat, threads, Facebook; even if a backup only adds 15k-100k views/mo it yields net-new paid subs you can attribute with a tracking link — distinct from the creator's existing audience. Post daily/multiple times, organize content a week in advance.

sources.quote: we set up hundreds of Instagram accounts... you can run everything off the cloud
In none of these accounts' bios, we're putting that link... we're just funneling to other Instagram pages
if we have 200 phones for one creator, and we're literally pulling one subscriber per day from each of the phones, then that's 200 subs a day
are you actually creating new accounts? Like... a second, third, fourth, fifth Instagram account... an extra 15, 20, 30, 100,000 views per month, it's better than nothing.
you can have a tracking link and all of a sudden you can show the client... extra 20, 50, 100 paid subscribers just from these backup accounts.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has a mature AI-account warmup system for AI personas (§3/§4) using GrapheneOS Pixels and Publer, but this is for synthetic creator accounts, not a satellite-funnel network for real creators. Palm has no documented equivalent for real-creator traffic amplification (§4 🔲 GAP — real-creator warmup/cadence not formalized; §5 🔲 GAP — traffic strategy not documented).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Evaluate adopting a scaled cloud-phone funnel for real creators. Palm's existing hardware-isolated warmup infrastructure (GrapheneOS, SIM inventory, Publer) gives a meaningful head start — the playbook could be adapted. Key decisions: (1) cloud phone provider selection, (2) automation tooling for feed warm-up, (3) funnel topology (satellite → main IG → OF). Start small (20–30 accounts for one creator) before scaling to 200+. Flag as high-priority given current IG instability signals.

consensus_risers.title: First DM: short curiosity-piquing message focused on a specific observable problem

variants.claim: The opening DM should be short, reference one specific problem visible on the creator's profile (e.g., wrong link-in-bio, no reels), and pique curiosity — never a long copy-paste paragraph.
variants.what_they_do: Agents identify a concrete, observable weakness on the creator's profile before messaging, then write a brief DM that names that problem implicitly or asks a curiosity-driving question. No feature-dumping or price-quoting in the first message. Outreach is sent from a personal IG account even with as few as 150 followers.

variants.claim: The first DM should establish authority by identifying a specific problem in the creator's setup rather than pitching the agency, and should funnel toward a no-pressure discovery call rather than explaining the revenue split.
variants.what_they_do: Template opener: 'Hey [Name], I came across your socials across a few platforms and noticed a few things in your setup that might quietly be hurting conversions.' Then a brief authority statement. Never mention the split in DMs — save that for the call. On the call: ask current earnings, show what's possible, then close on next steps and send a contract via Dropbox.

variants.claim: Structure the first cold DM to intrigue, spark curiosity, and end with a clear call-to-action, then move to a call instead of selling in DMs.
variants.what_they_do: ICC framework: first 4-5 visible words must intrigue (no 'hey hope you're having a great day'), then say something that piques curiosity, then a CTA (reply / send number / book a call). Don't treat DMs as a Q&A; get to the point and get them on a call. Type every message manually, personalize lightly, space sends (~20-30/hr, ~400-500/day across many accounts), and follow up daily for up to 2 weeks ('the money is in the follow-up').

sources.quote: focus on the problem that specific model has. So this could be the lack of posting reels, the wrong link in bio
Hey Name, I came across your socials across a few platforms and noticed a few things in your setup that might quietly be hurting conversions
Don't explain the split on the DMs. Obviously, explain the split on the actual call itself
I stands for intrigue. C... stands for curiosity and the next C stands for call to action.
what you're trying to do is get their phone number and call them as soon as possible.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented outreach message framework or script (§6 GAP). First-contact approach is entirely at Josh's discretion.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Create a first-DM template library: short (1–3 lines), profile-specific observation + curiosity hook, no agency pitch. Test from a personal or agency IG account. This is a direct complement to Palm's existing pipeline — once a creator is signed the content engine (§1) kicks in immediately.

consensus_risers.topic: signing call vibe and red flags
consensus_risers.title: Treat the signing call as a two-way interview with hard non-negotiables

variants.claim: Operators sign more durable creators by qualifying them on the call against work-ethic and commitment non-negotiables instead of signing whoever replies.
variants.what_they_do: Opens the call human-first (camera on, 'how are you', hobbies) to break barriers, then qualifies: rejects short-term-money motives and 'overly involved' creators, requires committed daily hours and a fixed content/live cadence, and walks away if the vibe or non-negotiables fail.

variants.claim: Sign only creators who fit a defined ideal-client profile and screen out predictable red-flag situations rather than chasing anyone with a link in bio.
variants.what_they_do: Ideal client: already on OnlyFans, has/open to a niche, posts consistently, communicates, pays on time, has some IG/Twitter/TikTok/Reddit presence, and is coachable. Qualify briefly in DMs then on a call. Red flags that get declined or probed: under 3-4 months on OF, about to travel/move/have a baby, just had a breakup, churned through 5+ agencies recently, know-it-all who rejects all advice, asks zero questions, takes weeks to sign.

sources.quote: work ethic trumps everything
have non-negotiables and have some respect for yourself
we prefer it's between 5 and six because then that allows a lot more content to be made
Here's my ideal client checklist. Number one, already on Only Fans... willingness to actually create content consistently... communication... payments... a bit coachable.
if they've gone through tons of agencies, especially in the last like 3 to 6 months... probably the common denominator is the creator.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm acquisition is Josh-DM-led with no documented signing-call qualification framework (§6 THIN); this is a build-toward script Palm lacks.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Adopt a documented signing-call SOP: human-first rapport, explicit work-ethic/cadence non-negotiables, and willingness to reject misaligned creators.

consensus_risers.title: Cold outreach is a volume-consistency-conversation game

variants.claim: Cold outreach signs creators predictably only when run as a system of high volume, long consistency, and value-first conversation rather than clever one-off scripts.
variants.what_they_do: Commits to 100 personalized DMs/day for 100 days (25/account across 4 accounts), value-first openers via voice/video note, and finding the avatar through conversation (10-30% qualify) rather than pre-filtering on the profile; built a 500k/mo agency on cold outreach before running a single ad.

variants.claim: Outreach should be tracked stage-by-stage in a daily spreadsheet so the bleeding step is fixed one variable at a time after a meaningful sample.
variants.what_they_do: Logs source link, DMs sent, replies, qualified convos, asks-for-call, booked/showed/closed per channel; waits ~3,000 DMs before changing anything, then changes one variable at a time. Uses a 7-12 message value-first follow-up chain (most deals close on touch 5-7) including a 'red flag' breakup follow-up with ~95% response.

variants.claim: The single biggest lever that broke Ellis past $30K/month was an acquisition system that never stops, not better marketing or chatting.
variants.what_they_do: Ellis runs a three-component acquisition machine (lead-magnet content + paid ads + a specific outreach method) combined with VSLs, a funnel, a call-setting service, and a closer — all running simultaneously and never paused even when onboarding a new creator. The system converts models continuously without founder involvement.

sources.quote: the real unit of measurement is 100 personalized DMs a day for 100 days
voice and video cut through 10 times better than text
after about 3,000 DMs... you'll know which layer of your system is actually bleeding
Most deals happen between the fifth and seventh touch point
the only thing that took us past it, it wasn't marketing, it wasn't chatting, it was getting a solid acquisition system that never stops running

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm acquisition is Josh-DM-led but not run as a tracked, committed-volume system (§6 THIN); no defined daily quota or multi-account structure.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Formalize Palm outbound as a committed daily-volume system (per-day DM quota, multi-account, voice/video openers, qualify-in-conversation) rather than ad-hoc DMs.

consensus_risers.title: Source creators across IG, Twitter, Reddit, TikTok, and prioritize referrals

variants.claim: Find creators by searching niche keywords across multiple platforms, but treat satisfied-client referrals as the highest-quality channel.
variants.what_they_do: Search 'only fans' / niche terms (e.g. gamer girl) on Instagram, Twitter, Reddit, TikTok and filter for link-in-bio + recent activity. TikTok surfaces up-and-comers; Reddit is where whales and creators already are. Best channel = referrals: after a great first month in the green, ask the happy creator who else does OnlyFans.

variants.claim: Creators typically use the same username on X/Twitter and Instagram, so you can search 'only fans' on X, collect usernames, then find and DM the same accounts on Instagram.
variants.what_they_do: Operator searches the word 'only fans' on X to surface creator accounts, copies their username, pastes it into Instagram search, and DMs there. They also use Instagram's 'related accounts' graph — following lists, likers, and commenters of one known OF creator — to find unlimited additional prospects.

variants.claim: Creator acquisition works by finding help-seeking creators in social comment sections, outreaching across every platform with a curiosity-driven message, and pushing for a quick call rather than closing over DMs.
variants.what_they_do: Searches TikTok for OnlyFans-related videos and mines the comment sections for girls asking for help (a signal they lack an agency), outreaches on every platform she's on with a short curiosity message ('100 fresh paying subs every single day'), and immediately pushes to a 5-minute call.

sources.quote: first one of course is Instagram... Your other option is going to be Twitter... The next one is going to be Reddit... You can also find them on Tik Tok.
by far the best is referrals... after you've delivered a great service... ask, do you know of anybody else who's also doing Only Fans.
you simply go on X and you search the word only fans on the search bar... Go on to Instagram and copy and paste the same username
within the comment section will be girls doing Only Fans asking for help
you basically just want to push for a call straight away

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm already uses referrals (§6) but has no documented multi-platform sourcing process or a systematic referral ask.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Document a multi-platform sourcing process and bake a structured referral ask into the post-first-month creator-success cadence.

consensus_risers.title: An AI chatbot can replace much of the chat team at a fraction of the cost

variants.claim: A well-configured AI chatting bot can handle 24/7 fan conversations and PPV upsells at a fraction of a chat team's cost, but only if it is carefully set up and trained.
variants.what_they_do: Uses Super Creator's 'Izzy' AI chatbot (~$200-300/month vs a full chatting team), configured with extensive persona data, slang, pricing strategy, and trained on rapport/texting/selling scenarios; warns that a bad setup just creates 'a shitty chatter'.

variants.claim: The emerging chatting meta is a hybrid where AI handles new-subscriber volume and identifies real spenders, then hands those fans to human chatters for long-term girlfriend-experience retention.
variants.what_they_do: Has AI run all new subscribers and complete the first script to separate spenders from time-wasters, then moves real spenders to a human chatter who gives a daily girlfriend experience to a small client list (~20 fans each); reports ~70% unlock rate and a single whale spending $50k in 4 months.

variants.claim: AI chatbots are improving rapidly and will soon deliver low-latency, high-quality chat at a fraction of the cost of even the cheapest human VAs, making human chatting teams economically unviable.
variants.what_they_do: The consultant flags AI chat as an imminent market force that will undercut human chatters (including cheap Filipino VA teams) on both cost and latency, framing it as a structural shift rather than a distant future trend.

sources.quote: it only costs like $2 $300 a month, which is pennies compared to how much you're forking out... on chatters
if you don't set it up correctly, you are simply going to make a shitty chatter
I will let the AI deal with all the new subscribers... so that it can differentiate between the actual spenders and the time wasters
we want the chatter to specifically just focus on a specific number amount of clients

AI chat bots are making huge improvements very quickly and will soon become a dominant force in the market, delivering low latency, high quality chat at a fraction of the cost of even the cheapest of Filipino virtual assistants

palm_comparison.vs_us: Directly maps to Palm's active AI-chatting project (§8 build-toward): defines a concrete tool pattern and the heavy-configuration requirement.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Evaluate a configurable AI chat bot (Super Creator-class) for Palm's AI-chatting project, budgeting for heavy persona setup/training rather than expecting plug-and-play.

consensus_risers.topic: Chatting ratio KPI and chatter hire threshold
consensus_risers.title: Target 1:10 sub-to-chat revenue ratio; hire chatters only after reaching $2–5K/month agency revenue

variants.claim: A 1:10 ratio of subscription revenue to DM/tip revenue is the core chatting KPI, and chatters should not be hired until the agency earns $2–5K/month to ensure they're affordable.
variants.what_they_do: Chatting ratio defined as: for every $1 in subscription revenue, target $10 in DM+tip revenue. Benchmarks: 1:6 = decent, 1:8 = good, 1:10 = great, 1:15 = excellent. Hire VA chatters at $3–$5/hour + commission once agency hits $2–5K/month. Manage chatters via Discord. Welcome message framework: thank sub, ask their name, build rapport → send teasers → sell PPVs → upsell. Chatting is treated as a strategy, not ad-hoc.

variants.claim: The ratio of subscription revenue to messaging/tip revenue is the single most diagnostic KPI for chatting quality — a 1:3 ratio signals poor chatting; a 1:6 ratio signals elite chatting on the same traffic.
variants.what_they_do: Agency uses the sub:messaging ratio as their primary audit metric when taking on new accounts. Baseline poor performance example: $8K subs, $23K messaging = 1:2.9 ratio. After installing their systems: $8K subs, $53K messaging = 1:6 ratio — same subscription base, same traffic, no new content. They claim the ideal target is 1:4 to 1:6. They present a concrete case study: account went from $31K/month to $62K/month purely from chatting system changes, adding $30K/month net profit. They also frame it as a loss quantification tool: a creator doing $10K/month with a 1:1 ratio is 'leaving $10K+ on the table monthly.'

variants.claim: Chatting performance should be measured as a ratio of message/tip revenue to subscription revenue, with elite teams targeting roughly 1:9 to 1:10.
variants.what_they_do: Calculates (messages + tips) / subscriptions; reports turning a ~1:1 ratio creator into 1:10+, and uses the ratio gap on creator calls to show lost revenue (e.g. a 1:3 vs 1:9 difference of thousands/month).

sources.quote: for every $1 you have in the chats, you want to have $10 in the or in the subs. You want to have $10 in the chats. So we stick to 1 to 10
once you're paying or making 2 to 5K a month, hire a VA to chat. So, if you're making, you know, 1K a month, 700 bucks a month, do not hire chatters
it should be a 1 to 4 or 1 to 6 ratio. Ideally the pay should be $4,000 from subscriptions, 16 to $24,000 in messages and tips
the ratio for messenger subscription is 5.98 times basically a 1:6 ratio… the total net earnings went from 31,000 in February to 62,000
add up your messages and tips as well and divide it by your subscriptions

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's chatting is fully outsourced (§8) with no internal KPI, ratio target, or hire-threshold documented. This is directly relevant to Palm's ~1yr roadmap to bring chatting in-house and the active AI chatting project. The 1:10 ratio is a concrete benchmark Palm should adopt as a performance target for the external team today and the in-house team when built.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Immediately: share the 1:10 sub-to-chat ratio benchmark with the external chat team as a performance SLA and track it in the existing Fan Tracker / whale-hunting infrastructure (§9). Build-toward: use the $3–$5/hour + commission pay structure and Discord management model as the template for in-house chatter hiring. Add 'chatting ratio' as a tracked metric in the Airtable reporting layer.

consensus_risers.topic: Urgency and exclusivity framing for PPV
consensus_risers.title: Apply time-sensitive + VIP-exclusivity framing to every PPV offer

variants.claim: Framing PPV offers as time-limited and personally exclusive ('only for you') reliably increases conversion rates.
variants.what_they_do: Two distinct psychological levers are scripted: (1) urgency — 'only available for a few hours'; (2) exclusivity — 'I don't usually send this to just anyone but you're special.' Both are delivered conversationally so they feel organic, not like a sales blast.

variants.claim: Setting up a PPV in advance — by mentioning a dream, fantasy, new outfit, or story — gives chatters a credible, personalized reason for sending the video later, making it seem far more valuable than a cold send.
variants.what_they_do: Chatter plants an anchor in DMs (e.g. 'I just had the craziest dream about doing XYZ with you in my green lingerie') or on the feed (e.g. 'just got this new outfit, what do you think?' with a photo). They do NOT sell immediately. They return 6, 12, or 24 hours later referencing the anchor ('I couldn't help but make that dream a reality — I put on that green lingerie and…') and send the PPV. The delay is what makes it feel authentic rather than scripted. Feed-level anchors (outfit post, emotional story post) can hit many subs simultaneously, with chatters following up individually in DMs.

sources.quote: make the offer feel exclusive and time sensitive example I have something special for you but it's only available for a few hours
I don't usually send this to just anyone but you're special I love talking to you
we can set up pay-per-views in advanced in the DMs or on the main feed... this should act as lore for the pay-per-view, which gives you a reason for sending it later
that delay between when you bring up the anchor and when you go into sell it is what makes it seem authentic

palm_comparison.vs_us: No internal PPV pricing or sales-framing baseline exists at Palm (§7 GAP). The external chat team may or may not use these frames; Palm has no oversight of that language.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Include both urgency and exclusivity language templates in the PPV script library Palm will build for its in-house chat team. Also share these frames with the current outsourced team as a performance-improvement brief. Track conversion rate before/after to validate.

consensus_risers.title: PPV Looping: Follow up within 5–10 seconds of any purchase to chain sales

variants.claim: The best time to sell a subscriber another PPV is immediately after they make any purchase, capitalizing on the 'f*** it' impulse to keep the buying momentum going.
variants.what_they_do: Chatters monitor their CRM (e.g. Infloww) for any purchase notification — old PPV, mass PPV, feed post unlock — and follow up within 5–10 seconds with a curiosity-hook message before the fan even finishes watching what they bought. The follow-up doesn't reference the old video; it teases the next thing ('I was literally just about to do something I don't usually do'). From that reply, the chatter plans to sell 5 sequential PPVs, each progressively more explicit or longer, so each one appears more valuable and justifies a higher price. This loop restarts every time the fan buys.

variants.claim: After the first PPV purchase, cycling between descriptive text exchange, free teasers, and the next PPV sell maximizes session revenue; stopping the moment the fan finishes or runs out of money protects the long-term relationship.
variants.what_they_do: Post-first-purchase loop: (1) ask 'what would you do now if you saw me like this?' to get descriptive text, (2) mirror his fantasy back, (3) insert free teaser (photo/voice/short clip — not enough to satisfy), (4) set up next PPV with yes-ladder, (5) sell. Repeat until fan orgasms or explicitly says no money. At that point, immediately pivot to relationship-building mode — pre-recorded 'had a great time' appreciation video, romantic post-session questions.

sources.quote: the best time to sell something to a subscriber is right after they've made a purchase
chatters know the second they get that notification on their inflow or whatever CRM you use, that they need to follow up with the subscriber instantly
descriptive text, sound turned on, get descriptive text from him, make him imagine the scenario in his head, send free teasers, set up the next PPV, and sell the PPV
Sell until you can sell, right? Until he's tapped out or he comes.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's DM monetization is fully outsourced (§8). There is no internal SOP, chatter workflow, or CRM trigger process documented. This is a concrete, mechanics-level system that would form the core of Palm's future in-house chat team playbook.
palm_comparison.recommendation: When building the in-house chat team, make PPV looping the foundational selling framework. Define the CRM trigger (purchase notification), set a hard response-time SLA of <30 seconds, write curiosity-hook templates for the follow-up message, and require chatters to plan a 5-PPV ladder before entering any loop. Build this into chatter onboarding SOPs.

consensus_risers.title: Three-stage follow-up protocol for unopened/unpurchased PPVs — guilt-trip only as last resort

variants.claim: When a fan doesn't buy, a structured follow-up sequence (curiosity ping → reason discovery → resolution → guilt-trip last resort) recovers sales without burning the relationship.
variants.what_they_do: Stage 1 — curiosity ping if left on seen: 'Where did you go?' / 'Don't just stare at it, you're making me nervous.' Stage 2 — if he replies but won't buy, ask why (trust issue, no money, bad timing) and resolve the specific objection (offer AI verification tip, suggest waiting). Stage 3 — only if he refuses to explain and is likely a time-waster: guilt-trip ('you made me film it and I'm not getting anything').

variants.claim: Sending a lone sad-face emoji to a subscriber who has gone quiet triggers guilt and reliably provokes a response, re-opening the monetization window.
variants.what_they_do: When a subscriber has not replied to a previous message, instead of sending follow-up texts or more content pitches, the chatter sends only a '😢' or '🥺' emoji. Bjorn claims ~99.9% response rate. The mechanism: guilt-trips the subscriber into explaining their absence, which restarts rapport and spending. No spamming — one emoji only.

variants.claim: Sending multiple unanswered messages in a row appears overwhelming and spammy, actively pushing subscribers away and reducing future engagement.
variants.what_they_do: The VA training guide explicitly prohibits sending additional messages if a subscriber has not responded. One message sits unanswered → chatter holds. The only permitted follow-up is the sad-face emoji tactic (single message). This is a firm SOP rule, not a suggestion.

sources.quote: If he left you on scene, you want to follow up, but don't be negative yet. Don't guilt trip. Use that as the last resort.
you want to find out why he isn't buying and resolve it

if a subscriber has not replied to your previous message and you want to get his attention simply just send a sad face emoji it is amazing how this will work I would say this probably works 99.9% of the time

do not spam subscribers with messages if they're not responding try not to send additional messages if the sub is not responding as this can appear overwhelming and spammy and could potentially put the subscriber off

palm_comparison.vs_us: No internal baseline (§8, outsourced). Palm has no documented follow-up or objection-handling flow.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Build this three-stage ladder into the in-house chatter SOP with explicit scripts for each stage. Flag time-wasters in the Fan Tracker (§9) after Stage 3 failure so chatters deprioritize them per the subscriber-priority queue (see finding on emoji tagging below).

consensus_risers.title: Write immersive, boner-test PPV descriptions that sell the fantasy before the sub sees the content

variants.claim: Long, vivid PPV descriptions that pass a 'can you picture it with your eyes closed' imagination test and a 'does it arouse' boner test dramatically increase unlock rates because the sub is buying stimulation from the description itself, not the preview.
variants.what_they_do: Chatters are required to write out full descriptions for every PPV — not 'here's a pic of my boobs babe' but a multi-sentence, immersive description of camera angle, positioning, what the model is doing, and what the sub should imagine. Two quality tests: (1) Imagination test — can someone visualize exactly what is in the content just from the words, with no preview? (2) Boner test — does the description itself arouse? If a male chatter reads it and finds it hot, it will likely work. Pre-writing descriptions for an entire set before the selling session begins is standard practice. This is treated as a core skill, not an afterthought.

variants.claim: Rather than describing the video's explicit content directly (which sounds repetitive and generic), chatters sext about an unrelated fantasy the creator 'has been having,' escalate it fully, then link the PPV as what the creator made while thinking about that fantasy.
variants.what_they_do: Chatter chooses a fantasy (threesome, free use, femdom, cheating, etc.) — ideally one the subscriber has shown interest in — and opens with 'there's something I've been thinking about but I'm embarrassed to say.' They build out the fantasy in full descriptive detail, getting the sub emotionally invested. When the sub is clearly engaged, the chatter pitches the PPV: 'I made this video thinking about doing that for the first time.' They connect specific moments in the video to the sexting ('at 2:41 I was thinking about what you said about…'). Fantasies are ranked from least to most explicit; each PPV in the loop uses the next fantasy up the ladder. This gives chatters an inexhaustible, varied pitch mechanism that doesn't repeat.

sources.quote: they are not vivid they do not stimulate the subscriber's imagination
does the description vividly describe what is in the content… will the description give the sub a boner when they read it
an alternative to that is sexting as if you were opening up about a fantasy that the creator has been having
you keep talking about the fantasy until they're clearly into it, and then you link that to the next pay-per-view saying something like, I made this video thinking about doing that for the first time

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no internal PPV copywriting standard (§2 THIN, §7 GAP). This is directly actionable: even before building an in-house chat team, Palm could create a PPV description template and writing guide for the external chat team and for creator-facing content briefing.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Create a PPV description writing guide with the two-test framework (imagination + boner test) and worked examples contrasting bad ('here's a pic babe') vs. good (fully immersive). Deliver this to the external chat team now as a quick win. When building the in-house team, make PPV description writing a standalone scored exercise in the pre-training filter — candidates who can't write a vivid description after coaching should not progress.

consensus_risers.title: Build every PPV script around a named fantasy scenario, not just an outfit or body part

variants.claim: ~50% of the reason subscribers buy on OnlyFans is entertainment/fantasy — not sexuality — so PPV content framed around a relatable fantasy scenario (e.g. 'friend's mom,' 'getting caught') dramatically outsells generic lingerie or body-part content.
variants.what_they_do: They reverse-engineer top-performing adult content categories, identify the 20+ fantasy archetypes that dominate those charts (step-family, public, caught, first-time GFE, etc.), then write every PPV script and hotkey set around one named fantasy rather than an outfit or physical attribute. The physical visuals are treated as the baseline; the narrative context is treated as the sales driver. They claim the same creator + same setup converts significantly higher when the PPV has a story context vs. a plain description.

variants.claim: Long, vivid PPV descriptions that pass a 'can you picture it with your eyes closed' imagination test and a 'does it arouse' boner test dramatically increase unlock rates because the sub is buying stimulation from the description itself, not the preview.
variants.what_they_do: Chatters are required to write out full descriptions for every PPV — not 'here's a pic of my boobs babe' but a multi-sentence, immersive description of camera angle, positioning, what the model is doing, and what the sub should imagine. Two quality tests: (1) Imagination test — can someone visualize exactly what is in the content just from the words, with no preview? (2) Boner test — does the description itself arouse? If a male chatter reads it and finds it hot, it will likely work. Pre-writing descriptions for an entire set before the selling session begins is standard practice. This is treated as a core skill, not an afterthought.

sources.quote: almost 50% of the reason they buy… it's the entertainment
the first action step is to rebuild your content around fantasy and storytelling
they are not vivid they do not stimulate the subscriber's imagination
does the description vividly describe what is in the content… will the description give the sub a boner when they read it

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's OF vault/PPV system is THIN (§2) — no documented PPV scripting, content framing, or fantasy-ladder structure. Palm's social content engine (§1) is data-driven and strong, but that pipeline is for top-of-funnel social reels, not OF vault/PPV sequencing. This finding is squarely in the gap.
palm_comparison.recommendation: When building out Palm's PPV system (§2 gap), define a fantasy taxonomy (20+ named scenarios) and require every PPV set to be assigned to a fantasy archetype before scripting. Treat outfit/body as the 'foundation' variable and fantasy narrative as the 'conversion' variable. Draft a PPV brief template: fantasy name, scenario setup, key dialogue hooks, hotkey copy. This should feed into the chatting team's PPV sequencing SOP.

consensus_risers.topic: Objection handling — add value before discounting
consensus_risers.title: When a subscriber objects to price, add more content first; only discount by removing content, not by lowering the price

variants.claim: Reflexively discounting when a subscriber says 'too expensive' trains them to always haggle; instead, offer more content at the same price first, and if a discount is unavoidable, remove content from the bundle rather than simply lowering the stated price.
variants.what_they_do: Rule 1 — Value-add first: if a sub resists price, add a small extra (bonus clip, picture set) and make it a huge deal, framing it as a personal favor. This gets full price paid and doesn't establish a discount expectation. Rule 2 — Renegotiation discount: if discounting is unavoidable, pre-load the PPV with a few extra pictures/clips by default. When the sub asks for less, remove those extras and drop the price — so the price reduction is tied to a concrete content reduction, not arbitrary. This preserves the perception that prices are meaningful. Rule 3 — Underpriced sub recovery: if a sub accepts a dropped price too readily, immediately propose a larger bundle ('I had an idea — I want to throw in extra stuff') to recapture upside.

variants.claim: When a fan objects to a custom content price, offering 50% upfront and 50% on delivery preserves the full price while reducing the perceived financial risk — avoiding the margin hit of a discount.
variants.what_they_do: Chatter expresses genuine excitement about the custom idea, adds her own suggestions to raise perceived effort/value, then quotes a premium price. On price objection: offer to deliver 'today/in 30 minutes' (urgency) and propose 50% now, 50% after delivery. Only drop the price as a final fallback if the 50/50 offer also fails.

sources.quote: it is a better first response to try and give them something small to sweeten the deal, and you get paid the full amount as opposed to just discounting
if they ask for a discount, you can take that stuff out and then drop the price, so you've now renegotiated
you can offer him 50/50. So he pays 50% before and 50% after
instead of dropping the price, you can tell him to pay half now, pay half later

palm_comparison.vs_us: No internal objection-handling framework exists at Palm (§8 outsourced). This is build-toward intelligence for both the in-house chat team and the AI chatting project, where objection-handling logic needs to be systematized.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Make 'add value before discounting, renegotiate via content removal' a non-negotiable chatter policy in the future in-house SOP. For the AI chatting project, encode this as a decision tree: objection detected → attempt value-add response → if second objection → remove content and lower price. Also document the underpriced-sub recovery move as a standard upsell trigger.

consensus_risers.title: Hard floor of 50/50 revenue split — never go lower

variants.claim: Agencies should refuse any deal below a 50% commission split, framing it as the clean, fair baseline rather than a negotiable starting point.
variants.what_they_do: States a non-negotiable 50/50 split as the minimum: if a creator earns $2K/month, the agency takes $1K. No tiered structures, performance bonuses, or sliding scales are mentioned — flat 50% is presented as the standard.

variants.claim: 50/50 is the standard baseline commission split for new OFM agencies, with room to take a higher percentage as expertise and results grow.
variants.what_they_do: Agency locks in 50/50 as the minimum split with every creator. As the agency proves its value and techniques mature, it renegotiates upward. No tiered structure described — purely experience-based progression.

variants.claim: Charge around 50% for full-service management rather than discounting to a low percentage to win clients.
variants.what_they_do: Treats 50/50 as industry standard for doing everything (marketing + chatting); explicitly warns beginners against charging 20-30% out of desperation. Nuance: when starting with sub-$1k creators, stay around 40-60% (default 50%); higher 30-40% can fit only specific larger cases. Logic: getting a creator 1k->10k is harder than 37k->120k, so don't give away margin on the hard early work.

sources.quote: don't go below 50/50 revenue split. If your model makes $2,000 a month, that's 1,000 for her and 1,000 for you. Clean, fair, and simple.
you say let's partner up, I'll take 50%, you take 50%, this is the benchmark, this is the baseline
charging like 20, 30, 35% when you're just starting out... the industry standard, go with 50% and you can't go wrong with that.
a 50/50 deal is quite common in the only fans management industry.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm sets commission % per creator at onboarding but has no documented split rationale, floor, or tier model (§7 GAP). The commission field exists in the Creators table but the logic behind what percentage to set is unwritten.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Document Palm's commission policy explicitly: establish a floor (50% is industry-standard per this and other sources), define whether Palm ever takes less (e.g., for a high-revenue creator where 50% would be disproportionate) and codify the rationale. Add this to the creator onboarding wizard as a displayed policy, not just a blank field.

consensus_risers.title: Free-trial subs, monetize later via PPV — not on the sub price

variants.claim: Operators run free or near-free subscriptions to maximize entries, then make essentially all revenue from in-DM PPV and tips rather than the subscription fee.
variants.what_they_do: Run a 7-day free trial (or $3.99 floor sub) with scarcity framing ('limited spots / offers left'); the sub itself is treated as a bonus while the real money comes from getting fans inside and selling price-locked PPV scripts, tips, wall content and a recurring 'girlfriend experience' (e.g. $500/week VIP).

variants.claim: Legitimate agencies take no money upfront and no hidden fees, charging only via the revenue split, which is itself a trust differentiator at signing.
variants.what_they_do: Takes nothing upfront and no hidden fees, contrasting against agencies that make creators 'buy their way in'; frames the relationship as partners not assets and lays all terms out from the beginning so they never change.

sources.quote: I strongly, strongly stress that you run a free trial. You don't charge for subs
the girlfriend experience
nothing up front... I've been approached by a lot of other agencies and they all wanted me to buy my way into the agency
we really strive on being partners, you know, and not just treating you like an asset

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented pricing/sub model (§7 THIN); chatting/PPV that this depends on is outsourced (§8).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Capture the low-friction-entry + monetize-in-DM model and a VIP/girlfriend-experience recurring tier as inputs to Palm's pricing model (§7) and in-house/AI chat roadmap (§8).

consensus_risers.title: Price dynamically off purchase history

variants.claim: Setting price per fan from their purchase history, backing off after a no and pushing higher for proven whales, beats one fixed price for all.
variants.what_they_do: Luca Pritchard uses dynamic pricing read from each subscriber's unlock history via tools like Infloww: if a fan stalled at $65 he offers 17/27/47/59 next time, but if a fan will pay $150-$200 for what others pay $20 for he sells at that willingness, and he keeps explicit content premium-priced so perceived value stays high.

variants.claim: Operators charge different prices for identical content based on the fan's traffic source and proven spend, deliberately under-pricing lower-quality traffic.
variants.what_they_do: X-sourced subs (lower quality, free subs) tagged and sold PPVs at roughly half the price of IG subs; proven spenders and whales quoted higher anchors; price never discussed openly, instead gauged from chat, fan notes and tip-baiting ('show me what you think I'm worth').

sources.quote: you pick a different selling strategy for every single subscriber
the low price might make him not want to buy instead of buy... keep the content premium priced
you can target them with lower price content to increase your spend per sub
we use differential pricing to target the X subs at lower lower prices

palm_comparison.vs_us: No per-fan price discipline or perceived-value rule at Palm (§7 THIN). Direct gap.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Adopt a dynamic per-fan pricing rule keyed to unlock history, plus a floor that keeps explicit content premium-priced to protect perceived value.

consensus_risers.title: Do every role yourself first, document it, then hand it off

variants.claim: Learn each part of the business personally and turn it into an SOP before delegating, so hires run a documented process.
variants.what_they_do: Owner did outreach/closing/onboarding/marketing/chatting himself first, then wrote an SOP doc + Loom video for each task so new hires self-train from the document instead of hours of live training. Hires for hunger/reliability/coachability over experience (experienced people resist the system). Tracks KPIs on every function (DMs sent, replies, calls booked, clients closed, revenue per chatter).

variants.claim: Operators master each posting channel personally before delegating it to low-cost VAs with strict SOPs, reserving founder time for high-ROI work.
variants.what_they_do: Run IG/TikTok yourself for ~1 month to learn it, then hand off to $1-3/hr VAs with documented SOPs and image/comment banks; keep TikTok geo-matched (US/UK/AU poster, not India/Africa) because TikTok pushes by poster location; X and Threads are easily outsourced, but the high-ROI anchor (IG/TikTok) is mastered first.

sources.quote: you funnel all of that traffic over to Only Fans. And then the second thing is the chatting.
One of the first things that you need to do is connect their Only Fans account to your Inflow.
easily outsource this to a VA for anywhere from a dollar to $3 an hour
master this better than anyone else can

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm is strong on productized content tooling (§1) but has no stated document-before-delegate discipline; team/org THIN (§10).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Standardize SOP + Loom documentation per role before backfilling it, hire for coachability, and attach KPIs to each function.

consensus_risers.topic: Role onboarding with daily winning formula checklist
consensus_risers.title: Give every new hire a 'daily winning formula' — a repeating Notion task with linked SOPs for each duty

variants.claim: A repeating daily task assigned to each team role, listing every responsibility with embedded Loom SOPs or email templates, eliminates guesswork, prevents the employee from inventing their own process, and lets the founder delegate without micromanaging.
variants.what_they_do: For each role (editor, CSM, appointment setter, etc.): create a Notion task template called '[Role] Daily Winning Formula' that repeats every weekday. Each checklist item links to an SOP (Loom video transcript pasted into Notion) or has a toggle with an exact email template. Example CSM items: 'Maintain daily updates in Notion by 10am', 'Review and QC all video content within 4 hours of submission', 'Send content to clients for approval', 'Update client portal'. Task auto-populates each morning. Pair with a 30-day onboarding course (gallery of Loom recordings) and pre-day-1 through day-6 onboarding checklist.

variants.claim: Without an SOP for every role, a new hire requires weeks of founder handholding, negating the time savings of hiring and blocking agency growth.
variants.what_they_do: Ellis and his partner documented every job into SOPs before delegating. They reinvested 100% of revenue for the first 12 months into the next hire, next system, or software — taking no personal salary. The founder's job is specifically to build the SOP so the hire can self-onboard and replace the founder in that function.

sources.quote: daily winning formula is basically what we'll do with this... I make it a repeating task every weekday... this is a task that just populates every single day for the client success manager
the reason your team would suck is because you're not actually equipping them with things like this to actually do well
every time I found myself doing something repeatedly, I basically just documented it, systematized it using Notion, and then trained someone else to handle it
if you do not have an SOP for every single part of your business, every single job, every single role, then again, you're never going to make it

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has Clerk roles (editor, ai_editor, chat_manager, creator) and an editor workflow with a day-counter task queue (§1, §10), but no documented daily winning formula, role-specific onboarding checklist, or SOP library linked to recurring tasks. The §10 gap notes no hiring criteria or scaling plan. This tactic is immediately applicable to Palm's editors and Amin (contractor posting role).
palm_comparison.recommendation: For Palm's two most active roles — editor and Amin (manual posting) — create a daily Airtable or Notion checklist with 5–8 items each, linked to existing SOPs (or quick Loom recordings). Specifically for the editor: items like 'check task queue, pull next 2 slots, upload to Cloudflare, submit for approval.' For Amin: 'check Telegram topic, post per schedule, reply /posted.' This is a low-effort, high-leverage move to reduce dependency on Josh/Evan for day-to-day direction.

consensus_risers.title: Loom-video SOPs to eliminate training misinterpretation

variants.claim: Recording yourself doing each task as a Loom video (rather than written docs) is the most reliable way to train hires and remove misinterpretation.
variants.what_they_do: Films a Loom once per task and reuses it forever; pairs Loom with backup documents for objections; uses live chatter monitoring on first 1-2 shifts plus 'trust but verify' checks for the first 1-2 weeks.

variants.claim: The fastest way to build an SOP library is to record a Loom of yourself doing the task, copy the auto-generated transcript into ChatGPT with a 'turn this into a step-by-step SOP' prompt, and paste the result directly into a categorized Notion SOP database.

Cultural rule: when a team member asks a question, don't answer it directly — create an SOP around it, send the SOP, and store it. The SOP library is filtered into role-specific views so each team member sees only their relevant SOPs.
