what we like to do is we like to use a tool called Loom, loom.com, and we just film a video on what we're doing. You go to the video, go to transcript, copy the transcript, turn this into a SOP
anytime somebody asks a question, what we're not doing is we're not just going to answer the question right from the jump. We're just going to create an SOP around it
these are all the SOPs. This is sales, client success, hiring, etc.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has SOP-style workflows in the editor/warmup UIs but no documented Loom-first training standard for new hires (§10). Cheap, high-leverage practice.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Standardize Loom-video SOPs for every Palm role (editor, poster, warmup operator, future chatters); store alongside the existing playbook editor.

consensus_risers.title: Automate contract → onboarding form → calendar booking via Zapier

variants.claim: The moment a client fills out a sales intake form, a fully automated chain fires: contract sent via e-signatures.io, welcome email triggered on signing, onboarding form delivered, and kickoff calendar link sent — all without manual intervention.
variants.what_they_do: Three sequential Zapier workflows: (1) Notion sales intake form submission → e-signatures.io contract creation with placeholder fields auto-populated; (2) contract signed → Gmail welcome email with onboarding form link; (3) onboarding form submitted → Gmail email with GoHighLevel calendar booking link. Appointment reminders sent at 24hr, 1hr, and 5min via GHL automation. All built in Notion with a Zapier Pro account ($29.99/mo) and e-signatures.io pay-per-use ($0.49/contract).

variants.claim: A repeatable onboarding sequence — contract → account access → OF account audit → marketing setup → expectations meeting — prevents early relationship failures.
variants.what_they_do: After contract signing (via PandaDoc or DocuSign), agency logs into creator's OF account (coordinating 2FA in real time), audits and organizes the subscriber list by spend tier, organizes the content vault, sets up missing social platforms (IG, TikTok, Twitter), sends a content schedule, and holds an expectations-setting conversation covering response windows, platform norms, and month-1/2/3 milestones.

sources.quote: e signatures.io, it's not a $30 a month type of subscription to send my contracts. It is you pay per contract sent
sales intake form to contract. So, that's what we want to do
contract signed. Send onboarding form. Perfect. I'm going to go back to my folder
first step is to of course do the onboarding right so the onboarding entails a couple of different things

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has a form wizard onboarding flow for creators (§6) and auto-generated PDF contracts via Puppeteer, but no documented automated chain that sequences contract → onboarding form → kickoff booking without manual steps. The creator-side onboarding is thin; there is no client/brand-side onboarding OS equivalent documented in Palm's baseline.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Build the same three-Zap chain for Palm's creator onboarding: sales intake form submission (Airtable or a form) triggers contract PDF (already Puppeteer-built), signing triggers an onboarding packet with a kickoff Calendly/GHL link, and form completion triggers a Telegram or email alert to Josh. Low incremental cost given existing Puppeteer contract system.

consensus_risers.topic: Loom-to-SOP pipeline for building a scalable SOP library
consensus_risers.title: Record a Loom, paste the auto-transcript into ChatGPT, and get a step-by-step SOP in minutes

variants.claim: The fastest way to build an SOP library is to record a Loom of yourself doing the task, copy the auto-generated transcript into ChatGPT with a 'turn this into a step-by-step SOP' prompt, and paste the result directly into a categorized Notion SOP database.
variants.what_they_do: Loom.com recording → copy transcript → ChatGPT prompt → formatted SOP pasted into Notion SOP library, tagged by department (sales, client success, hiring, organic marketing, etc.) and status (draft/active). SOPs are linked from daily winning formula tasks and role onboarding courses via hyperlinks. Cultural rule: when a team member asks a question, don't answer it directly — create an SOP around it, send the SOP, and store it. The SOP library is filtered into role-specific views so each team member sees only their relevant SOPs.

variants.claim: Every time the founder instinctively knows how they would handle a situation, that instinct should be written into an SOP so the team follows the same standard without requiring the founder's involvement.
variants.what_they_do: TDM's framework: 'your intuition, the way you feel, should become the rules of the business.' If the founder would handle a creator issue a certain way, that becomes a documented framework the team is trained on. The goal is to remove the founder from execution while preserving their operating philosophy.

sources.quote: what we like to do is we like to use a tool called Loom, loom.com, and we just film a video on what we're doing. You go to the video, go to transcript, copy the transcript, turn this into a SOP
anytime somebody asks a question, what we're not doing is we're not just going to answer the question right from the jump. We're just going to create an SOP around it

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented SOP library (§10 gap). The Loom-to-SOP method is extremely low-friction and matches Palm's current AI-assisted workflow culture.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Start Palm's SOP library with three highest-leverage SOPs: (1) editor submission-to-approval workflow, (2) Amin Telegram posting protocol, (3) creator content filming brief format. Use Loom + ChatGPT as described. Store in Airtable or a Notion page linked from the admin portal. Target: 10 SOPs in 2 weeks, then assign new SOPs as a recurring task whenever a process question is asked more than once.

consensus_risers.topic: Monthly NPS survey system with action protocol
consensus_risers.title: Send a monthly NPS form to every active client and require a response to every score

variants.claim: Sending a net promoter score survey to all clients on the first of every month, routing responses into a tracked database, and requiring the CSM to respond to every score — including passives and detractors — directly improves retention and provides data to improve the product.
variants.what_they_do: Notion form (NPS survey) with name, email, score (0–10), and open comment. Repeating task on the 1st of each month assigned to CSM with the form link and a pre-written email template embedded in the task. On submission: Notion automation sends internal team email alert and a thank-you email to the client. Scores roll up to the client OS (average NPS per client visible at a glance). CSM board shows unresponded scores filtered by 'responded = unchecked.' Company-wide average NPS tracked on client success dashboard. Threshold: any score below 8 triggers immediate action review.

variants.claim: A recurring weekly Zoom/video call with the model — showing results, flagging problems honestly, and requesting content adjustments — is the primary relationship-maintenance mechanism.
variants.what_they_do: They schedule a standing weekly call per model on a video platform (Zoom or equivalent). The call covers: current account performance update (good and bad news), requested content changes for the coming week, and any operational issues. The visual face-to-face element is cited as relationship-strengthening beyond what async messaging achieves.

sources.quote: NPS. It's called net promoter score. Detractors, passives, promoters. We want to set up a system where we can send this out once per month
we don't want somebody to fill out an NPS form and then we don't respond
if this is below 8, below 9, I need to focus on this. I need to like dial this in like really really hardcore
set up a Cadence of calls like on a weekly basis where you get on a zoom or get on some sort of call with the model you see them face to face and you explain what's going on

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has a Fan Tracker CRM and whale-hunting tools (§9) for fan retention, but no documented NPS or satisfaction survey system for creator retention. The §9 gap notes no written retention/churn/win-back strategy. This tactic maps directly to that gap — applied to Palm's creators rather than brand clients.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Implement a monthly creator satisfaction pulse (can be a simple Airtable form or Typeform): 1–10 score, one open question ('what could we do better?'), and one win-capture question ('any recent milestone we should celebrate?'). Route responses to Airtable, auto-alert Josh on any score below 7, and have a 48hr response SOP. This costs near-zero and directly addresses the §9 retention gap.

consensus_risers.title: Classify every creator A/B/C and allocate resources accordingly — cut C creators

variants.claim: Most agencies carry too many low-quality ('C') creators out of fear of losing gross revenue, which silently caps growth by stealing attention from better creators.
variants.what_they_do: Dylan classifies creators into three tiers: A = high upside, consistent content, coachable, scalable brand; B = potential, needs structure, worth developing; C = inconsistent, ignores feedback, creates chaos, low upside, drains team. A creators get premium attention and resources. B creators get active development. C creators get fixed, downgraded, or removed. The diagnostic question is: 'Knowing what I know now, would I sign this creator again today?' If no, there must be a reason she is still consuming company resources. He notes a 30k/month agency with 2 great creators beats a 100k/month agency with 14 nightmares.

variants.claim: Low-tier models produce low-tier results regardless of agency skill, so raising the minimum quality bar of creators signed is a prerequisite to scaling past $50K/month.
variants.what_they_do: Ellis explicitly rejects creators with low work ethic regardless of other factors. He qualifies inbound leads using story-driven case-study content (e.g. 'how we took a creator from 0 to 40K') that pre-filters serious creators, rather than vanity result screenshots. The content itself does the qualification work before any call.

sources.quote: the creator audit is where you classify your roster into A, B, and C
knowing what I know now, would I sign this creator again today? That is the last question. That's very important.
most agencies carry way too many Creators, and they keep them out of the fear of losing their revenue number
if you keep acquiring low tier models, you're going to get low tier results because you got low tier work ethic and it's not going to scale

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has a `Creators` table with status tracking and onboarding workflow (§6), but no documented creator-tier classification, no systematic review cadence, and no written policy on when to downgrade or drop a creator. This is a GAP (§9 retention, §7 pricing/deal structure). Palm's creator acquisition is currently reactive/Josh-DM-led (§6) with no quality filter documented.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Implement a quarterly creator audit in the admin portal: list all active creators, assign A/B/C rating based on gross profit, content compliance rate, team hours consumed, and a 'would sign again?' flag. Set explicit policies: A creators get priority chat team allocation and content pipeline slots; B creators get a 60-day improvement plan; C creators get a downgrade notice or off-boarding. Pair with the profitability scorecard (finding above) to make ratings data-driven rather than subjective.

consensus_risers.topic: Model churn risk and single-model dependency
consensus_risers.title: Audit creator concentration risk — no single creator should represent >20% of net profit

variants.claim: If one creator leaving cuts agency revenue by 30–40% or more, the agency is not a real business — it is gambling, and concentration in a single creator is the most common structural failure at the $20–50K/mo level.
variants.what_they_do: Renato uses a live coaching-call example: a client with ~18 models had his top model leave the day before a call, immediately wiping out the majority of his net profit. Renato's prescription is to structure the agency so no single creator departure is catastrophic — achieved through (a) owning traffic assets, (b) salary-model deals, (c) enough creator diversification, and (d) a replicable onboarding/growth system so any creator can be replaced quickly.

variants.claim: Because Instagram accounts, OF accounts, and individual creators can all disappear overnight without warning, any agency with revenue concentrated in a single asset is one ban or personal decision away from zero.
variants.what_they_do: Agency explicitly flags concentration risk as a daily operational concern: monitors the revenue share of each creator, each Instagram account, and each employee. If one creator generates the majority of revenue, the agency treats that as a red flag and accelerates recruiting to dilute the dependency. Same logic applied to Instagram accounts (build more traffic sources, have a ban-response protocol) and key employees (have them document and train backups before they leave).

sources.quote: his main client left him. And she — I think he has around 18 models and she was his main model. And she left the day before our call. And so most of his net profit and revenue just went away
if one model leaving cuts your revenue in half or 40% or a lot, your agency isn't structured in the correct way.
Systematize everything. Have a structure. If you don't, if one model leaves, you're fucked. That is not a good agency structure.
You have one creator that makes the majority of the revenue, that should be a fucking red flag. That should scare the hell out of you.

palm_comparison.vs_us: §9 PARTIAL — Palm has whale-hunting and fan-tracker CRM tools but no documented creator-level concentration-risk monitoring or churn signal process. There is no written policy on maximum revenue concentration per creator. §6 notes acquisition is Josh-DM-led with no systematic pipeline, which makes rapid replacement of a departing creator difficult.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add a creator concentration metric to the Palm admin dashboard: what % of total agency gross revenue does each creator represent? Set an internal alert threshold (e.g. >25% from a single creator triggers a diversification review). Pair this with an accelerated acquisition effort when concentration is high. Also codify a 'creator departure runbook' so the team has a response plan ready before it is needed.

consensus_risers.title: Give high spenders 1:1 personalized treatment, never mass messages

variants.claim: Retain top spenders by excluding them from mass blasts and giving escalating personalized touches as they cross spend thresholds.
variants.what_they_do: Tier fans by spend on a smart list; anyone over a threshold (e.g. $1,500) gets zero mass messages/PPVs, only personalized 1:1. Touches escalate: >$500 first night = personalized voice note (real or ElevenLabs AI); always call them by their saved nickname; >$2,500/mo = a short safe-for-work selfie video saying their name; occasional physical-prize raffles. One client made $56,500 in 30 days from her top 5 fans.

variants.claim: When managing multiple accounts simultaneously, chatters should prioritize responses to high-spending or long-tenure subscribers over the general queue.
variants.what_they_do: Chatters explicitly rank their open conversations: whales and loyal long-term members get first responses. Low-spend or new subscribers are handled after, often with templated messages to maintain efficiency without abandoning them.

sources.quote: for big spending fans. You don't send them mass messages or mass pay-per-views... you want to give them that one-on-one personal interaction.
Anybody over $500 in their first night should get personalized voice notes... we can use AI voice notes... 11 Labs.
If the fan has spent more than $2.5,000 in one month, record a short personalized safer work selfie video saying his name.
prioritize high value chats focus on subscribers who frequently spend money or longtime members

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has whale tooling (Fan Tracker, going-cold detection, smart-list-style data) but no written whale-nurture playbook (§9 PARTIAL/GAP); chatting is outsourced (§8), so the tactics are build-toward.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Turn whale data into an operating playbook: spend-threshold tiers, exclude-from-mass rule, nickname use, voice-note/selfie-video escalations (AI voice where applicable), raffles — build-toward for in-house/AI chat.

consensus_risers.title: Build a perpetual stockpile of karma-farmed Reddit accounts via VA team

variants.claim: Running Reddit at scale requires a dedicated VA farming team that continuously creates and seasons accounts to a minimum karma threshold so you always have ready inventory to rotate in when accounts get banned.
variants.what_they_do: Agency runs a team of VAs (plus automation) that create Reddit accounts daily, warm them for 2-3 days acting as normal users, then farm post karma (target: 2,000) and comment karma (target: 300) by posting in meme/advice subreddits. Accounts are stockpiled; when a posting account gets banned a seasoned replacement is pulled immediately from the pile. VAs are paid per account that hits the karma threshold. Automation now supplements VAs for scale.

variants.claim: Creating Reddit accounts from scratch and warming them organically produces longer-lasting accounts than buying, because device-fingerprint mismatches on purchased accounts trigger faster bans.
variants.what_they_do: Agency creates accounts fresh, runs a 3–5 day warmup (scroll, join subreddits, 1–2 upvotes/downvotes/comments per day), then begins posting. Bought accounts are acknowledged as faster but riskier. Each account gets a unique tracking/trial link, unique content (spoofed), and unique captions so no two accounts can be chain-banned via shared signals.

sources.quote: we have an account creation system every day. there's new accounts being created and there's just a farming team
An account is done seasoning if it hits 2,000 post karma and 300 common karma
we run the accounts on mobile… mobile Reddit is a lot safer and the accounts last a lot longer than they would on web
the link should be different every account… nothing should link a different account to another because that's how you can get chain banned

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented Reddit strategy at all (§5/§2 GAP — traffic/distribution not documented). This is pure build-from-zero territory. Palm's Apify scraping and Publer infra exists for IG/TikTok but nothing maps to Reddit account farming or karma seasoning.
palm_comparison.recommendation: If Palm adds Reddit to its traffic stack, do NOT start with black-hat scale. Begin with the expert's own minimum viable advice: one dedicated VA, one phone, one account per creator, organic posting to well-matched subreddits daily. Only invest in a farming pipeline after proving per-creator ROI. Document karma thresholds (2,000 post / 300 comment) as acceptance criteria for any farmed account.

consensus_risers.title: Scrape a 100k-subreddit master list keyed to creator physical attributes, then niche-match posts

variants.claim: Rather than manually searching for relevant subreddits, build a scraped database of 100,000+ subreddits tagged by niche attributes so any new creator can be immediately mapped to every viable subreddit based on her physical characteristics.
variants.what_they_do: Agency built (or uses) a scraper that pulls every subreddit and tags it by niche (ethnicity, body type, fetish, etc.). When a creator is onboarded, her attributes (blonde, big breasts, e-girl, etc.) are matched against the database to generate her posting list. Volume over precision: one piece of content gets posted across every marginally relevant subreddit. Key insight: NSFW content also gets cross-posted into SFW subreddits (makeup, car repair, GeoGuessr) using curiosity/cleavage tactics to reach users who have never enabled NSFW settings.

variants.claim: Maintaining curated subreddit lists segmented by niche — including a dedicated list of 200+ subreddits that accept posts with zero karma — eliminates the time drain of manual subreddit research and reduces the risk of posting in wrong/banned communities.
variants.what_they_do: Franko maintains 10+ lists, one per niche (Latina, milf, teen, boobs, feet, SFW, trans, cosplay, etc.). A separate list of 200+ zero-karma-required subreddits is used specifically for new accounts in the warmup-to-promo transition period. Team members pull the list matching the model's niche and post immediately. A master list of 2,500+ subreddits is available for further expansion.

sources.quote: what we would do is we would just have a scraper that would basically scrape every single subreddit that would or could be tied to either one of these niches. You'd build up a list of subreddits. You'd have a 100,000 subreddits

there's also a lot of say for work subreddits where people would just, you know, I don't know, ask for advice for piercings in their ears or or makeup advice. And you could also post in these subreddits
for new Reddit accounts who don't have karma yet, I've got a list of over 200 subreddits that need zero karma
I've got a giant list that I sat and made it myself. The list has subreddits for every single niche you want to run

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no Reddit presence (§5 GAP). Palm's content pipeline (§1) does use Apify scraping for IG inspiration, so the scraping capability exists — it would need to be redirected to Reddit subreddit discovery. The SFW infiltration angle is completely absent from any Palm workflow.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Commission a one-time scrape of Reddit subreddits tagged by adult-content niche to build a master lookup table. Store in Airtable alongside creator attribute fields. On creator onboarding, auto-generate a subreddit posting list. Prioritise NSFW subreddits first; layer in SFW curiosity subreddits (lifestyle, hobby) only once the core strategy is proven.

consensus_risers.topic: Reddit posting ramp — gradual volume increase
consensus_risers.title: Ramp Reddit posts from 1/day to 20/day over ~3 weeks to extend account lifespan

variants.claim: Starting at one post on day one and adding one additional post per day up to a cap of 20/day significantly extends account lifespan compared to posting at full volume immediately.
variants.what_they_do: Day 1 = 1 post, Day 2 = 2 posts, Day 3 = 3 posts, etc. Hard cap at 20 posts per day. Slower early ramp trades a few extra days of setup for months of account longevity. Posting beyond the ramp schedule is considered greedy and leads to early bans.

variants.claim: Any agency new to Reddit should start with the simplest possible setup — a dedicated VA with a phone-based account posting organically to well-matched subreddits — before investing in black-hat infrastructure, because even this baseline can generate 10+ paid subs/day.
variants.what_they_do: Expert's explicit minimum recommendation regardless of agency size: assign one VA per creator for Reddit, have them use a physical phone (not desktop — Reddit deprioritises desktop activity), post organically every day or every other day to keep the account warm, no scheduling software, no automation, no upvote manipulation. Find the right subreddits through manual trial first. This single-account approach can yield 10+ paid subs/day on a well-matched creator with hundreds of viable organic NSFW subreddits available.

sources.quote: I'd rather take a few extra days and keep the account alive for months then get greedy and have to start all over again
Day one, one post. Day two, two post. We just add one more each day until we hit 20 post a day max
get a dedicated VA. Not no scheduling, no post scheduling, nothing. If you're new to Reddit, get a dedicated VA… just posts every day in or every other day to keep the account warm
one account could definitely get you, let's say, anywhere upwards from 10 paid subs per day

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented Reddit posting cadence or ramp strategy (§5 GAP on traffic/distribution). Palm's social posting cadence is managed via Publer with jitter/rotation for IG/TikTok, but Reddit is not in scope yet.
palm_comparison.recommendation: If Reddit is added to Palm's traffic stack, build the 1→20 ramp rule into the Reddit SOP and, where possible, encode it as a task sequence in Airtable (similar to the AI warmup day-by-day task structure already in place) so editors/VAs execute it consistently without judgment calls.

consensus_risers.title: Create & organically warm Reddit accounts to reduce chain-ban risk

variants.claim: Creating Reddit accounts from scratch and warming them organically produces longer-lasting accounts than buying, because device-fingerprint mismatches on purchased accounts trigger faster bans.
variants.what_they_do: Agency creates accounts fresh, runs a 3–5 day warmup (scroll, join subreddits, 1–2 upvotes/downvotes/comments per day), then begins posting. Bought accounts are acknowledged as faster but riskier. Each account gets a unique tracking/trial link, unique content (spoofed), and unique captions so no two accounts can be chain-banned via shared signals.

variants.claim: Spending two weeks manually karma-farming — scrolling, joining subreddits, posting a few times, dropping one or two comments per day — prevents Reddit from flagging the account when promo begins.
variants.what_they_do: For the first two weeks after account creation: scroll a few minutes daily, join a variety of subreddits, upload a few normal posts, leave one or two genuine comments per day max. No promo content at all during this window. The goal is account age and karma score so posts are not auto-removed when promo starts.

sources.quote: we run the accounts on mobile… mobile Reddit is a lot safer and the accounts last a lot longer than they would on web
the link should be different every account… nothing should link a different account to another because that's how you can get chain banned
we karma farm manually for about 2 weeks before we even think about any promo
you shouldn't rush this process. If you try to skip this, Reddit will flag you and the account's dead

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented Reddit traffic system (§5 🔲 GAP — traffic/distribution strategy not documented). This is a concrete, zero-ad-spend channel Palm is not running.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Build a Reddit playbook: create accounts fresh, enforce per-account unique links + spoofed assets, run 3–5 day warmup, start at 1 post/day scaling to 20 subreddits over a month. Assign a VA to run and rotate accounts. Prioritize for real creators immediately; applicable to AI personas too once OF funnels are live.

consensus_risers.title: Bootstrap Threads from existing IG account for follower carry-over

variants.claim: Creating a Threads account from an existing Instagram account gives an immediate follower boost that accelerates early growth.
variants.what_they_do: TDM connects each creator's existing IG account to Threads so that existing Instagram followers automatically carry over at account creation, providing an instant audience base rather than starting from zero.

variants.claim: High early posting frequency on Threads accelerates follower growth because the platform still incentivises new content volume.
variants.what_they_do: TDM posts 5–8 times per day per Threads account in the first weeks. Once the account hits 1,000 followers (or after a couple of weeks), they reduce cadence to 3–5 posts per day. They mix carousel posts (highest performers) with shorter comment/text posts as filler.

variants.claim: Threads algorithmically amplifies brand-new accounts' first posts, making it faster to gain initial traction than Twitter/X, where momentum takes much longer to build.
variants.what_they_do: Create a new Threads account, post immediately — the first post gets organic reach from Threads' push. Content is simple engaging text (e.g. 'somebody FaceTime me' style posts targeting lonely men). Bio links to Telegram rather than directly to Fanvue, then Telegram DMs are used to warm and convert. Positioned as a 'hidden gem' due to platform newness.

sources.quote: use an account that you already have on Instagram to make the threads account because this will give you a boost, a kickstart from all the followers that you have coming over
5 to eight posts per day per account. Now, after a couple of weeks or when you reach 1,000 followers, you can back off a little bit to three, maybe five posts a day
Threads is really underrated. It can bring in a lot of traffic especially right now because it's more of a newer platform
when you make a brand new Threads account or get a Threads account right off the bat, your first post is going to get views

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented Threads strategy (§5 GAP — traffic/distribution not documented). Palm does run IG accounts for creators (§1) but there is no recorded practice of extending those to Threads.
palm_comparison.recommendation: When Palm sets up IG accounts for real creators, simultaneously create a linked Threads account to capture the follower carry-over benefit at no extra cost. Add Threads to the Phase 3 distribution rollout (batch-5).

consensus_risers.title: Two-funnel X system: paid retweet drops plus comment-baiting

variants.claim: X/Twitter is run as a deliberate dual funnel combining paid retweet-group 'drops' for reach and manual niche comment-baiting for targeted conversion.
variants.what_they_do: Funnel 1: pay a retweet-group manager to drop one image/day for 100-500 retweets; Funnel 2: build a comment bank plus 30-image SFW bank, follow ~1k niche accounts (warmed slowly, 5-10/hr), comment 50-100/day with short CTA captions under target posts; track via Fanvue tracking links targeting 3-5% click-to-sub CTR.

variants.claim: Outsourcing Reddit and X content distribution to a specialist contractor can yield disproportionate revenue relative to cost (cited: $1K/month spend generating ~$20K/month in revenue).
variants.what_they_do: Agency pays a Reddit/X specialist contractor roughly $1,000/month to distribute content across relevant subreddits and X accounts, funneling traffic to OF. The contractor is found through the agency's Telegram network. ROI is tracked as revenue attributed to that traffic source versus contractor cost.

sources.quote: I call it the twofunnel system
A good CTR is 3 to 5%
I pay him like a thousand bucks a month and he brings me like $20,000 a month
your Reddit and X contractors are distributing content and funneling into loads of subs turning into hefty profit

palm_comparison.vs_us: Traffic/distribution strategy beyond Publer/Telegram posting is an explicit gap (§5/§8); X is not in Palm's documented funnel.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Pilot an X comment-baiting plus tracking-link funnel for real creators as a low-cost channel and document CTR benchmarks; comment-bait mechanics transfer to real creators.

consensus_risers.topic: Instagram DM outreach volume and rate-limit management
consensus_risers.title: Send 50 IG DMs/day; back off 5 below rate-limit trigger

variants.claim: Sending ~50 DMs per day from a personal IG account is the optimal beginner outreach volume, and you should drop 5 below whatever number triggers Instagram's rate-limit warning to avoid restrictions.
variants.what_they_do: Operator DMs from their personal IG account targeting creators with 5k–50k followers who mention or have OnlyFans. They monitor for Instagram's 'Check community guidelines / actions required' warning; whatever number triggered it, they permanently send 5 fewer. Follow-up sequence: wait 24 hours after no reply, then send a voice message for personalization.

variants.claim: Sending 60 outreach DMs per day (10/hour, 6 hours/day, 7 days/week) produces 1,680 messages per month, which is sufficient to sign at least one client.
variants.what_they_do: Agency instructs new operators to hit exactly 60 DMs per day split into 6 one-hour blocks. At that pace, 1,680 monthly contacts makes signing a single client near-certain. They also recommend tracking every outreach in a spreadsheet and following up with non-responders.

sources.quote: I want you to send 50 DMs per day
always send five below. Whenever you get that message
wait 24 hours and then send another follow-up from that point on. a voice message
For 30 days, if you do six 60 outreach messages a day, you can do 10 per hour, six times a day

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's acquisition is Josh-DM-led with no documented daily volume target, rate-limit protocol, or follow-up cadence (§6 GAP). No internal baseline on outreach message structure or follow-up timing exists.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Codify a daily DM volume SOP: start at 50/day, instrument the rate-limit floor, and enforce a 24-hour → voice-message follow-up sequence. Document this in the acquisition runbook so it can be handed to a future acquisition VA.

consensus_risers.topic: Inbound creator acquisition via personal brand
consensus_risers.title: Build personal brand (YouTube + IG) so top creators DM you first

variants.claim: High-quality creators with good work ethic come inbound to agencies that have visible authority content (YouTube, IG reels), eliminating the need to chase them.
variants.what_they_do: Oliver posts OFM-focused reels on a dedicated IG account (~10K followers) and maintains a YouTube channel since 2023; creators DM him directly saying they're looking for an agency. He signs them on the first call. He explicitly states creators who DM you first already see you as an authority, while creators you have to chase tend to have poor work ethic.

variants.claim: The binding constraint on agency growth is obscurity, not lead supply, so building a known brand is the highest-leverage acquisition asset.
variants.what_they_do: Argues all growth is constrained by irrelevance; a brand compounds trust before the first conversation so a large share of pipeline becomes inbound, and reframes competition as obscurity rather than other agencies. Notes ~7M creators means the market is nowhere near saturated.

sources.quote: this girl called DM'd me on my OFM Oliver Instagram where I post a bunch of reels
the creators worth taking on…they usually come and find you. You don't try to find them.
your real constraint isn't leads. It's irrelevance. Nobody knows you exist
80% of my pipeline is from inbound, from my brand

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's acquisition is Josh-DM-led outbound (§6 — THIN). No documented inbound content flywheel. This is the opposite motion: competitor earns inbound by investing in public-facing authority content, Palm currently spends effort chasing creators.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Build a public-facing OFM authority presence for Palm — even a small dedicated IG account posting agency results/tips reels. This converts acquisition from outbound grind to inbound pull, and pre-qualifies creators (they arrive already trusting Palm). Start with one reel/week; track how many creator DMs it generates over 90 days.

consensus_risers.title: DM prospects from a personal-looking account, not an agency page

variants.claim: Run creator outreach from a personal-looking profile rather than a branded agency account so messages get read.
variants.what_they_do: Send from your personal/duplicated account (lifestyle pics, normal-person vibe), never an 'OFM manager' agency account, because models skim their request folder and skip anything that reads as an agency. Stop labeling yourself 'OFM manager' anywhere; call it 'talent/influencer management' instead so you aren't lumped in with the spam.

variants.claim: Setting up a credible Instagram profile with professional photos (not casual/topless) is the primary vehicle for cold-outreaching creators, and showing your face builds trust that converts to signed deals.
variants.what_they_do: Agency owner creates a personal IG account with polished location/lifestyle photos (real or Pinterest-sourced). Shows face for credibility; faceless is acceptable if IG photos are professional. DMs creators who appear stuck, plateaued, or post-bad-agency. Offers a free 1-week trial to prove value, then converts to 50/50. Moves promising leads to FaceTime to close.

sources.quote: most people send their messages out from like an agency account... I would actually highly advise you to do your personal account.
please stop saying OFM manager in either your username, your bio... try to be a little bit different... talent management agency.
you want to just make an Instagram profile, you want to set it up some nice business photos
you can offer if you find a model and you believe in yourself… I'll work for free for you for a week and if things improve then we can go to a 50/50 split

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm acquisition is Josh-DM-led + referrals with no documented outreach-profile standard (§6 THIN/GAP).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Standardize an outreach profile spec (personal-style account, lifestyle content, non-'OFM' positioning) as part of Palm's acquisition playbook.

consensus_risers.title: Test 3–5 branding angles simultaneously in first two weeks; double down on data winner

variants.claim: Running multiple branding angles in parallel in the first two weeks (different captions, framings, positioning — same creator, same account) generates real performance data fast enough to commit to a winner before competitors even finish their first test.
variants.what_they_do: In weeks 1–2 they ran three branding angles simultaneously on the same account (Swedish girl, Ukrainian girl, American girl in Dubai) — same creator, same videos, different captions + framing + marketing positioning. Ukrainian angle got 2.4× the views of the other two combined. They killed the losing angles within two weeks and rebuilt all downstream systems (chatting scripts, DM persona voice, mass messages) to match the winning brand identity within the same week — not a month later.

variants.claim: Content performance is governed by three levers — volume (negates luck), clarity (avatar's own language), and proof (converts attention to belief).
variants.what_they_do: Treats every post as a lottery ticket so quantity is the path to quality; insists on speaking the avatar's literal words ('tired of chatting all day') over marketing jargon; and backs claims with documented results. 'If you're not embarrassed by how much content you produce, you're not producing enough.'

sources.quote: in the first two weeks, we ran three branding angles through trials. We had a Swedish girl, a Ukrainian girl, and the American girl living in Dubai.
the back end just adds on top of it but fast…same week this shouldn't take you a month or two
volume negates luck... you are not in the content business, you're in the volume business
clarity beats cleverness... speak your avatar's language

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's content pipeline (§1) uses inspo data to decide what to film but has no documented mechanism for parallel branding trials. Palm's onboarding doesn't include a defined brand-testing phase. This is a high-signal gap: Palm could run branding trials using its existing scheduling infrastructure (Publer) without new tooling.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add a 'branding trial phase' (days 1–14) to Palm's creator onboarding: define 3 angle hypotheses based on inspo analysis, produce a small batch of content per angle, post in rotation, and set a data gate at day 14 (views, comments, shares, profile visits). Build the winning angle's identity into an Airtable 'Creator Brand Card' that downstream systems (chatting scripts, mass message tone, PPV captions) reference. Target: 3–5 branding tests per creator per quarter.

consensus_risers.title: Treat every revenue-generating asset as likely to disappear tomorrow; diversify across creators, accounts, and traffic sources

variants.claim: Because Instagram accounts, OF accounts, and individual creators can all disappear overnight without warning, any agency with revenue concentrated in a single asset is one ban or personal decision away from zero.
variants.what_they_do: Agency explicitly flags concentration risk as a daily operational concern: monitors the revenue share of each creator, each Instagram account, and each employee. If one creator generates the majority of revenue, the agency treats that as a red flag and accelerates recruiting to dilute the dependency. Same logic applied to Instagram accounts (build more traffic sources, have a ban-response protocol) and key employees (have them document and train backups before they leave).

variants.claim: If one creator leaving cuts agency revenue by 30–40% or more, the agency is not a real business — it is gambling, and concentration in a single creator is the most common structural failure at the $20–50K/mo level.
variants.what_they_do: Renato uses a live coaching-call example: a client with ~18 models had his top model leave the day before a call, immediately wiping out the majority of his net profit. Renato's prescription is to structure the agency so no single creator departure is catastrophic — achieved through (a) owning traffic assets, (b) salary-model deals, (c) enough creator diversification, and (d) a replicable onboarding/growth system so any creator can be replaced quickly.

sources.quote: You have one creator that makes the majority of the revenue, that should be a fucking red flag. That should scare the hell out of you.
his main client left him. And she — I think he has around 18 models and she was his main model. And she left the day before our call. And so most of his net profit and revenue just went away
if one model leaving cuts your revenue in half or 40% or a lot, your agency isn't structured in the correct way.
Systematize everything. Have a structure. If you don't, if one model leaves, you're fucked. That is not a good agency structure.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's AI persona infrastructure already provides meaningful diversification (multiple accounts, hardware-isolated, §3/§4 — MATURE). However, on the real-creator side, creator concentration risk is not formally tracked (§9 — PARTIAL; §7 — GAP). There is no documented ban-response protocol or creator-departure contingency plan in the codebase.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add a 'revenue concentration' metric to the agency health dashboard: flag any single creator, IG account, or traffic channel that exceeds 40% of total revenue. For the top-concentration creator, document a written contingency plan (which backup account takes traffic, who covers DMs, how fast can recruiting replace volume). For IG accounts, formalise the ban-response protocol currently referenced but not codified.

consensus_risers.title: 7-day IG warm-up: algorithm training, niche engagement, delayed link

variants.claim: New social accounts are warmed for about a week of human-like activity and niche engagement before any adult link is added, to build trust score and avoid early bans.
variants.what_they_do: Day-by-day: matching Gmail/name, scroll+like niche reels to train the algorithm, neutral (non-model) profile pic, follow ~15 niche creators/day, engage and like older US/UK 'simp' commenters under competitor posts to pull them over (never DM), then post first reel ~day 7 and only add the link.bio funnel 2-4 weeks in when followers justify the risk.

variants.claim: Bought aged IG accounts need only a 3–5 day warmup (engaging OF creator posts, scrolling) before going live — longer warmups recommended by others are unnecessary in practice.
variants.what_they_do: Purchase aged IG accounts to skip karma/history building. Warmup = 3–5 days of scrolling and engaging with OF creator content only. Then set up bio with link.me or Linktree, profile photo (face + slight chest), pin three posts showing key body parts relevant to niche. Post 2 Reels/day. For account isolation: UK/US/AU/CA creators can use home Wi-Fi unless ban rate exceeds ~10 accounts; otherwise use VPN/proxy.

sources.quote: how you set up and warm up an Instagram properly within 7 days to avoid being banned
Build up your Instagram account slowly and patiently
we buy our accounts aged and then we do a warm up for around like… 3 to 5 day warm up of just interacting OF creators engaging with the posts scrolling and that's it
IG is not as strict as people think it is… if you're in the UK, US, Australia, Canada… then you can use mobile Wi-Fi

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's AI warmup is mature and fully specified (§4 90-day cadence) but real-creator warmup is a documented gap (§4 THIN); this gives a concrete short-form warmup playbook.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Adapt a documented short warm-up + delayed-link checklist for new real-creator IG accounts (algorithm training, niche engagement, no-DM rule, risk-to-reward link timing) to fill the §4 real-creator gap.

consensus_risers.title: Every reel needs hook, retention, reward; 2-3/day across content pillars

variants.claim: Reels are the primary growth lever and each is engineered with a 3-second hook, mid retention and a payoff, produced at 2-3/day split across a few fixed content pillars.
variants.what_they_do: Treats reels as the top of the IG pecking order (reels > posts > stories > funnel); structures each as hook (first 3s) / retention / reward (e.g. an ugly-to-hot transformation); defines ~3 niche content pillars and split-tests them at 2-3 reels/day, doubling down on winners and cutting losers; moved from static text-on-image reels to 'clean motion' video.

variants.claim: Posting 2–3 Reels per day split between algorithm-maintenance lifestyle content and virality-engineered vertical content is the primary Instagram growth method for driving OF subscriptions.
variants.what_they_do: 2–3 Reels/day. Filler content = lifestyle/personal content to keep the account active and algorithm-fed. Vertical content = explicitly engineered for virality — Snapchat-style verticals, talking-head videos, mirror videos. Both types use 'link in bio' and pinned comments directing to OF. Competitor analysis is central: identify top-performing competitor videos, isolate the 3 best, test posting similar styles, then use performance data to teach the creator what to film and why it works (lighting, facial expression, movement).

sources.quote: Every reel should have a hook, a retention and a reward
a minimum of two quality reels per day
Filler content is just going to be her lifestyle. This keeps the algorithm happy… Vertical content is what brings new people into your page
Find three competitors. Make a list of the top performing videos. Take the three most performing videos. Put that in a style selection process

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's inspo pipeline scores viral reels and decides what to film (§1 MATURE) but does not codify per-reel hook/retention/reward structure or a pillar split-test cadence; real-creator cadence is a gap (§1).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add a reel-structure standard (hook/retention/reward) and a pillar-based split-test posting cadence to the inspo pipeline output for real creators; complements Palm's existing scoring engine.

consensus_risers.topic: Emotional experience framing vs. transactional selling
consensus_risers.title: Sell the intimate experience, not the content — fans pay for emotion, not pornography

variants.claim: Subscribers don't spend money on OF because they can't find free explicit content; they spend because they're buying an emotional, intimate experience that free sites can't replicate.
variants.what_they_do: Chatters are trained to frame every interaction as building genuine intimacy — shyness, excitement, escalating personal connection — rather than a content transaction. The chatter script is designed so the fan feels like the experience is happening *with* the creator, not being sold a product. The script's pacing (slow → fast) mirrors real intimacy. This psychological framing is positioned as the core reason scripted chats outperform both mass PPVs and one-off custom content.

variants.claim: The girlfriend experience (parasocial connection, scripted live-feeling sells, no PPV spam) drives the highest and most durable chatting revenue.
variants.what_they_do: Optimizes the OF wall to feel like 'Snapchatting,' uses 7-10 stage scripts with verification images, never initiates sexual content (lets the fan initiate), handles broke fans with ego-protecting lines, and forbids PPV spamming across the whole team.

sources.quote: only fans isn't about sexuality it's about emotions and experiences
what he really searches for are the emotions the real sexual experience not just the pure sexuality
the GF experience, the only thing that I try to run in OFM

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's chatting is fully outsourced (§8) with no internal script or framing doctrine. This 'experience over transaction' principle should anchor chatter training philosophy when Palm builds in-house. Also directly applicable to AI chatting — arguably more so, since AI chatters must compensate for lack of a real person.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Make 'sell the experience, not the content' the #1 principle in the chatter training SOP. Require chatters to spend a defined minimum number of exchanges on warmup/small talk before any PPV offer. For the AI chatting project, build emotional escalation cues into the conversation state machine — track where in the 'intimacy arc' each fan is before triggering a sell.

consensus_risers.title: Live-call training with seconds-fast feedback loops and single-point focus

variants.claim: The fastest way to improve a new chatter is to sit on a live call with them while they chat, give feedback within seconds, and focus on fixing only one thing at a time.
variants.what_they_do: Manager joins a live call with the chatter while they work in real time. Feedback is immediate ('stop, do it again'). Manager identifies the single highest-leverage improvement point and works only on that until fixed, then moves to the next. Chatters are not 'saved' — manager tells them what to do but makes them execute it themselves so the learning sticks.

variants.claim: Running a live training session is an effective onboarding format for new chatters joining an OFM agency.
variants.what_they_do: The agency hosts a live group session with slides to train chatters, suggesting a structured, synchronous onboarding approach rather than purely async SOPs or recorded content.

sources.quote: Fast feedback loops. If you want someone to learn something quickly, you need there to be a short gap between when they do something and when they get feedback on it. And I mean seconds
Don't step in and do it for them. You can tell them what to do. That's how they actually learn
I want to take this chance to officially welcome you to our super live session

palm_comparison.vs_us: No internal chatter training exists (§8, outsourced). Build-toward.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Adopt this as the core training protocol for first 2–4 weeks of any new in-house chatter. Build a simple tracker: one 'current focus point' per chatter updated weekly so managers don't multi-task the coaching.

consensus_risers.topic: subscriber list segmentation for PPV targeting
consensus_risers.title: Segment subscriber lists by content preference and renewal status to send targeted PPV messages

variants.claim: Using OF's native list feature to segment fans by content preference (feet, striptease, etc.) and renewal status enables personalized PPV sends that feel direct and increase conversion.
variants.what_they_do: Creates named lists inside OF (beyond defaults): VIP list for high-paying subscribers ($200/mo tier who get PPV free), preference-based lists (feet fans, striptease fans, etc.). When sending a PPV mass message, selects the relevant list as the target or excludes VIP list from paid messages. Sends preference-matched messages to niche lists with personalized copy to increase perceived intimacy.

variants.claim: Sending the same mass message to all subscribers burns money; segmenting by purchase behavior and sending tailored tone, CTA, and price to each segment dramatically increases conversion.
variants.what_they_do: Minimum one mass message per day (online or standard). Lists segmented into at minimum: new subscribers, OC (old/churned) list, active selling list (have purchased content), big spenders, no-spenders, time wasters (high engagement, zero purchase). Each segment receives different message tone, CTA, and price point. A 500-dollar spender gets a completely different pitch than a subscriber who joined 8 hours ago. Iteration loop applied to mass messages: if view rate is high but reply rate is low, convert statements to questions ('Would you rather see me in the bikini or out of it?') — reply rate doubles.

sources.quote: you can also create lists for certain people like if you know there are certain people that really like feet pictures or there's certain group of people that really like striptease videos you can add them to that list and then whenever you do want to send out a certain type of message you can send it to everybody
We segment the lists. new subscriber list, OC list, active selling list…big spenders, no spenders, time wasters
someone who's already paid 500 bucks doesn't need the same pitch as someone who just joined 8 hours ago

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's fan intelligence layer (§9) has a Fan Tracker CRM and whale-hunting module but no documented PPV targeting by content preference. Chatting is outsourced (§8) so the external team may not be segmenting sends. This is build-toward for both the in-house chat team and the AI chatting project.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Brief the external chat team to begin using OF list segmentation immediately: at minimum, maintain a 'VIP/renewing' list excluded from paid PPV, and build 2–3 preference lists per creator based on DM history. Feed preference data back into the Fan Tracker CRM (§9). When building the in-house chat SOP, make list maintenance a weekly chatter task. For the AI chatting project, model preference tagging as a fan-profile attribute driving automated PPV selection.

consensus_risers.title: Five-tier chat priority queue: active sext session first, time-wasters last

variants.claim: Organizing the subscriber inbox into five explicit priority tiers ensures chatters always work highest-revenue conversations first and never waste time on non-spenders while whales wait.
variants.what_they_do: Priority order: (1) fans currently in a sext/purchase session, (2) fans being transitioned into a session (teasing phase), (3) new subscribers (unknown spend potential — test them), (4) known spenders/whales in relationship-building, (5) time-wasters — only addressed when inbox is empty. Agency adjusts exact ordering based on where their revenue concentrates.

variants.claim: When managing multiple accounts simultaneously, chatters should prioritize responses to high-spending or long-tenure subscribers over the general queue.
variants.what_they_do: Chatters explicitly rank their open conversations: whales and loyal long-term members get first responses. Low-spend or new subscribers are handled after, often with templated messages to maintain efficiency without abandoning them.

sources.quote: number one in my opinion for everybody should be the ones that are currently in a sexting session
The last ones should be time wasters because they're just wasting our time
prioritize high value chats focus on subscribers who frequently spend money or longtime members

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has a whale-hunting module (§9) and Fan Tracker CRM but no documented real-time chat prioritization queue. The outsourced chat team may have their own system, but Palm has no visibility into it.
palm_comparison.recommendation: When building the in-house chat team, implement this queue as a visible status layer in the Fan Tracker CRM. Consider surfacing active-session and new-subscriber flags in the whale-hunting dashboard (§9) so the chat manager can monitor live priority distribution across creators.

consensus_risers.title: Agency founder must chatter personally before hiring chatters

variants.claim: Before delegating DM monetization to hired chatters, the agency operator must handle messaging themselves to understand fan psychology and what drives spending.
variants.what_they_do: Requires the founder to personally reply to DMs in the early stage — not as a permanent task but as a learning phase. The stated goal is to internalize why fans message, why they send money, and what emotional triggers drive PPV conversions, before writing SOPs or hiring people to do it.

variants.claim: Agency owners should personally do DM chatting for two weeks to understand fan psychology and upsell mechanics before hiring chatters, and should prioritize South African hires over Southeast Asian for native English slang and cultural alignment.
variants.what_they_do: New agency operators personally handle all DMs for roughly two weeks to learn what works. They then hire chatters from South Africa (preferred) or South America, paying in local currency (favorable exchange rate). Three chatters per creator account rotate 8-hour shifts for 24/7 coverage. Chatters are trained by the operator who now understands the product firsthand.

variants.claim: Founders who chat themselves get short-term quality gains but sacrifice the bandwidth needed to get results, attract better creators, and scale.
variants.what_they_do: Ellis explicitly stops chatting himself and redirects that time into training chatters to surpass his own skill level. He frames chatter training as a higher-leverage use of founder time than doing the chatting, because it unblocks bandwidth for content direction, acquisition, and results that attract higher-tier creators.

sources.quote: You need to understand the psychology. Why is this dude, you know, typing a message this girl online? Why is he here? Why is he sending money? You need to understand these things.
step two, is to hire chatters... the vast majority of our team is from SA, from South Africa. Mainly because English is their first language
if you are chatting yourself, you're not going to have that bandwidth — the key there is to train your chatters to become better than you

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm currently outsources all chatting to an external team (§8) and has no internal SOPs, scripts, or fan psychology documentation. This means Palm's leadership may lack the ground-level insight needed to evaluate chatter quality or build in-house scripts for the roadmap in-house chat team.
palm_comparison.recommendation: As Palm moves toward an in-house chat team (~1yr roadmap, §8), leadership should run a time-boxed hands-on chatting sprint (e.g., 2-4 weeks on 1-2 accounts) to build firsthand understanding of fan psychology before writing scripts or hiring. Document findings as the foundation of Palm's internal chatting SOP.

consensus_risers.title: Three chatters + reliever for seamless 24/7 shifts

variants.claim: Per-creator chatting is staffed with three eight-hour chatters plus one reliever, with documented handoffs for seamless 24/7 coverage.
variants.what_they_do: Runs 3 chatters (six days) + 1 reliever (three days), all 8-hour shifts; Discord clock-in/out, end-of-shift reports, and at-next-chatter handoff messages so in-progress conversations transfer without gaps.

variants.claim: Adding a dedicated chat supervisor from day one — who reviews 50–60 conversations daily — catches revenue-destroying patterns weeks or months before they'd surface in KPI dashboards, preventing compounding subscriber loss.
variants.what_they_do: On day 1 they assign a 'Hulk supervisor' whose sole job is to pull and deep-analyze 50–60 chats per day. The supervisor checks: script adherence, objection-handling consistency, list management, correct content routing to fans. When within 7 days a pattern of subs dropping after first PPV was spotted, the supervisor flagged it to the backend manager, they reviewed PPV strategy for the week, adjusted price and script. Three chatters on 8-hour shifts give 24/7 coverage; all conversations answered within 70 seconds. Supervisor is added before problems appear — not as damage control after.

sources.quote: Three chatters and one reliever
we add the supervisor as an asset on day one…the supervisor pulls 50 to 60 chats a day and analyzes deeply
they catch the patterns of the chatters fast…within 7 days of the switch…the supervisor noticed that was like a chunk of subs that were dropping off after the first PPV

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm outsources chatting and has no internal shift model (§8). This is a concrete staffing template for the future in-house team.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Adopt the 3+1 eight-hour shift model with Discord clock-in/EOS reports as the staffing blueprint when Palm builds its in-house chat team.

consensus_risers.title: Track which message templates drive the most sales and circulate winners across the team

variants.claim: Systematically monitoring which specific messages generate the most engagement and sales, then sharing winners as team templates, compounds chatter performance over time.
variants.what_they_do: Chatters are instructed to set daily sales targets, track which message styles and scripts convert best, and collaborate with teammates to share high-performing templates. If a flirty line is working across multiple fans, it gets codified as a reusable template for the whole team.

variants.claim: The most effective chat scripts are built bottom-up from live testing — chatters freestyle, track what gets positive responses and high PPV buy rates, and promote those lines/setups into the official script iteratively.
variants.what_they_do: The agency does not hand chatters a finished script to copy-paste. They provide the structural framework (rapport → passive tease → yes train → PPV ladder → gratification close) and then chatters freestyle within it. When a specific line or PPV setup consistently produces positive fan responses or high buy rates, it gets promoted into the agency's canonical script. The script is therefore a living document refined by actual conversion data.

sources.quote: if a certain flirty message is working well for multiple subscribers you can use it as a template for future chats
If we see that a certain sentence has positive feedback from most subscribers, we will use it in the script. If a PPV has a high buy rate with a certain PPV setup, we will use it in the script
how we create scripts in our agency is we get on the page, we start chatting and see what works

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no internal chat performance tracking or template iteration loop (§8 OUTSOURCED). The outsourced team is a black box for this kind of feedback. Palm's content pipeline (§1) does this well for social content (viral scoring, promote-best) but the same logic has never been applied to chat.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Apply Palm's existing 'scrape → analyze → promote best → reuse' logic from the content pipeline (§1) to chat scripts: have the outsourced team (and eventually in-house) log top-converting messages weekly, review them in a shared doc, and build a living script library. This is a direct structural parallel to a system Palm already operates well.

consensus_risers.title: Gate chatter hires on English, typing speed, and connection before training

variants.claim: Screen chatters on measurable competencies before hiring and training, since bad chatting silently destroys revenue.
variants.what_they_do: Before hiring a chatter, run an English skill test, a Wi-Fi speed test, and a words-per-minute test; only those who pass get hired and trained. Bad chatters (spelling/grammar errors, mispriced first PPVs like $4.99 or $200) are a top-7 agency-killing mistake.

variants.claim: Any person with strong communication, fast typing, and basic psychological intuition can be trained to elite-level chatting in 1–3 weeks if screened correctly and put on pure commission alignment.
variants.what_they_do: Three hard requirements: (1) strong English/communication; (2) fast typing speed (response target: under 5 minutes, ideally under 2); (3) baseline understanding that emotion drives sales. Intangible green flags: natural rapport-builder, curiosity about psychology, prior sales experience, hunger/drive. Sourcing: Upwork, OnlineJobs.ph, Telegram OFM groups (warn: screenshot fraud common there), personal referrals, own personal brand. Screening: Tally/Google form with basic info + scenario role-plays showing real fan messages — evaluate for naturalness, sales awareness, human-sounding responses. This step eliminates 80% of low-quality applicants. Compensation: agency now runs 100% pure commission at 10% of sales — no base pay. Rationale: protects downside on non-performers, aligns incentives, self-filters lazy chatters. Previous hourly/base structures cost the agency 'tens of thousands' in losses from underperforming chatters.

sources.quote: we give them an English skill test... a Wi-Fi speed test... a words per minute test. Only once they check out on all of these, that is when you hire them.
anyone with right fundamentals can be trained to a top tier chatter in one to two weeks
we personally favor now healthy commissions… 10% of sales is what they get

palm_comparison.vs_us: Chatting is outsourced (§8) so Palm doesn't run this screen today; it's build-toward intelligence for the planned in-house chat team's hiring SOP.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Build-toward: when standing up the in-house chat team, adopt a pre-hire screen (English / WPM / connection) and a first-PPV pricing standard.

consensus_risers.topic: Price integrity / repackaging instead of discounting
consensus_risers.title: Never fold on PPV price — repackage content instead of discounting

variants.claim: When a fan doesn't purchase at the stated PPV price, cutting the price cheapens the brand; instead, trim or repackage the same video at a lower price point so it appears to be different content.
variants.what_they_do: If a fan ignores or declines a PPV, the chatter does not reduce the listed price. Instead, they clip the same video to a different length, change whether a thumbnail is shown, or rebundle it, then re-pitch it as a 'different video you might like' at a lower price. This preserves the perceived value of the original piece.

variants.claim: Reflexively discounting when a subscriber says 'too expensive' trains them to always haggle; instead, offer more content at the same price first, and if a discount is unavoidable, remove content from the bundle rather than simply lowering the stated price.
variants.what_they_do: Rule 1 — Value-add first: if a sub resists price, add a small extra (bonus clip, picture set) and make it a huge deal, framing it as a personal favor. This gets full price paid and doesn't establish a discount expectation. Rule 2 — Renegotiation discount: if discounting is unavoidable, pre-load the PPV with a few extra pictures/clips by default. When the sub asks for less, remove those extras and drop the price — so the price reduction is tied to a concrete content reduction, not arbitrary. This preserves the perception that prices are meaningful. Rule 3 — Underpriced sub recovery: if a sub accepts a dropped price too readily, immediately propose a larger bundle ('I had an idea — I want to throw in extra stuff') to recapture upside.

sources.quote: if there's a point where they don't purchase don't fold on your pricing what you can do though is you can trim videos make them different lengths repackage it so it doesn't seem like you are folding
it is a better first response to try and give them something small to sweeten the deal, and you get paid the full amount as opposed to just discounting
if they ask for a discount, you can take that stuff out and then drop the price, so you've now renegotiated

palm_comparison.vs_us: No internal PPV pricing model or price-integrity policy is documented at Palm (§7 GAP). This is a concrete tactic for the in-house chat and pricing buildout.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add a 'no-discount, repackage only' rule to the future chat team SOP. Build a small asset library of clip variants (different lengths, thumbnail on/off) for each PPV set so chatters have ready-made repackaged versions to offer.

consensus_risers.title: Set hard minimum PPV prices: $19.99 images, $39.99 videos, $300 customs

variants.claim: Selling PPV content below ~$20 for photos and ~$40 for videos trains fans to see the creator as cheap and destroys long-term monetization.
variants.what_they_do: This agency enforces hard price floors: no image PPV under $19.99, no video PPV under $39.99, no custom under $300. Low pricing is treated as a disqualifier, not a conversion tool. The logic is anchoring — cheap prices signal low value and attract fans who won't spend more, wasting chatter time for minimal return (framed as bad 'cash-for-effort' ratio).

variants.claim: Pricing PPV content by video length with a hard floor of 1 minute and a practical ceiling of ~$20 maximizes purchase rate across different fan spending levels.
variants.what_they_do: Short or clothed videos: under $8. Longer or more explicit videos: $8–$20. Hard rule: never charge for content under 1 minute — that goes free. Photos posted free to subscribers (subscription covers them); photos only charged if custom. Sends a mix of price points so lower-budget fans still convert on cheaper items while high-spenders can hit the $20–$25 ceiling.

sources.quote: you're now going to be selling pictures for no less than $19.99 each
Customs I'm not joking no less than $300

if I send out a shorter video or like a twerking video or I'm clothed then it's somewhere under like eight dollars and if it's a little bit more than that then it's somewhere over eight dollars always under I would say twenty dollars

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented PPV pricing model or price floors (§7, explicit GAP). No internal baseline exists for what creators should charge for images, videos, or customs.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Establish Palm's standard PPV price floor as agency policy: ~$20 minimum for images, ~$40 minimum for videos, ~$300 for customs. Document this in the creator onboarding materials and share with the external chat team as a standing instruction. This is a zero-cost, immediate revenue lever.

consensus_risers.title: Commission-plus-bonus comp structure with daily ranked leaderboard drives consistent chatter output

variants.claim: A commission-only base combined with milestone bonuses, daily ranked leaderboards shared with the team, and regular chat reviews sustains chatter performance over time and is the structural reason revenue grew from ~$20k/month to $80k+.
variants.what_they_do: Daily: an assistant compiles each chatter's individual revenue and ranks them on a leaderboard shared with the whole team — publicly feeding ego and competitive instinct. Comp structure: commission only plus bonuses tied to achievable milestones (the presenter spent three days designing this mathematically; result was a ~25% revenue lift). Chat reviews: manager logs into accounts periodically, reviews a sample of conversations, records a screen-capture with verbal feedback, sends to the chatter. Every chat review correlates with a numbers increase. Pre-training filter used before formal training to screen candidates — only those who pass pre-training get to the actual training stage. Hiring criteria codified into a checklist (not all personality types are suited to the role).

variants.claim: Every team member should be financially incentivized by the growth of the specific business metric their role controls, so their personal interest and the agency's interest are identical.
variants.what_they_do: TDM structures compensation by role-specific output: media team paid on video performance, marketing team on traffic, chat team on sales closed, recruiting team on hires made. Each person wins when the business wins, but through the metric they personally control — not generic salary.

sources.quote: every single day I have my assistant compile a report of the rankings of all of the salespeople because I give out bonuses
my compensation and bonus structure which I believe is key to the continued motivation of my chatters… numbers have gone up about 25% as a result
media team are compensated for these videos doing well. Marketing team are compensated for more traffic. Chat team are compensated for more sales.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented chatter hiring criteria, comp structure, or performance management cadence (§8 outsourced, §10 THIN). This is the operational skeleton Palm will need when building the in-house chat team.
palm_comparison.recommendation: When scoping the in-house chat team build (~1yr roadmap per §8): (1) Design a commission + milestone-bonus comp structure before the first hire — do not default to hourly. (2) Implement a daily revenue leaderboard visible to the whole chat team. (3) Build a pre-training filter (written test + role-play) that candidates must pass before formal onboarding. (4) Schedule weekly chat reviews for each chatter, not just when numbers drop. These four structural elements can be planned now and should be in the in-house team design spec.

consensus_risers.title: Hire for coachability and reliability, not pre-existing chatting skill

variants.claim: The correct chatter hiring filter is competence, reliability, and honesty — not existing chatting skill — because a motivated person can be trained to top-level in 2–3 weeks.
variants.what_they_do: Explicitly de-emphasize prior chatting experience as a hiring criterion. Instead prioritize candidates who demonstrate effort, follow-through, and personal motivation throughout the screening pipeline. Agency trains chatters to top level within 2–3 weeks once the right person is selected. The entire multi-stage funnel (attention checks, situational tests, live calls, Loom compliance) is designed to surface coachable, reliable people — not to find pre-trained chatters.

variants.claim: Any person with strong communication, fast typing, and basic psychological intuition can be trained to elite-level chatting in 1–3 weeks if screened correctly and put on pure commission alignment.
variants.what_they_do: Three hard requirements: (1) strong English/communication; (2) fast typing speed (response target: under 5 minutes, ideally under 2); (3) baseline understanding that emotion drives sales. Intangible green flags: natural rapport-builder, curiosity about psychology, prior sales experience, hunger/drive. Sourcing: Upwork, OnlineJobs.ph, Telegram OFM groups (warn: screenshot fraud common there), personal referrals, own personal brand. Screening: Tally/Google form with basic info + scenario role-plays showing real fan messages — evaluate for naturalness, sales awareness, human-sounding responses. This step eliminates 80% of low-quality applicants. Compensation: agency now runs 100% pure commission at 10% of sales — no base pay. Rationale: protects downside on non-performers, aligns incentives, self-filters lazy chatters. Previous hourly/base structures cost the agency 'tens of thousands' in losses from underperforming chatters.

sources.quote: It's about you hiring competent, reliable, and honest people that you can teach the skill of chatting. Because we taught chatters in like two or three weeks to get to a top level.
anyone with right fundamentals can be trained to a top tier chatter in one to two weeks
we personally favor now healthy commissions… 10% of sales is what they get

palm_comparison.vs_us: No internal chatter hiring criteria (§8 outsourced). This philosophy should anchor Palm's future hiring rubric — it means Palm can source from a much wider talent pool and doesn't need to pay a premium for 'experienced' chatters.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Document 'coachability, reliability, honesty' as Palm's three core chatter hiring criteria. Build the scoring rubric for each screening stage around these three traits rather than chatting knowledge. This also reduces cost — experienced chatters command higher pay; trainable reliable people do not.

consensus_risers.title: Hire cheap offshore chatters but train them intensively and systemize the work

variants.claim: The cost-effective chatting model is hiring low-cost offshore chatters and de-risking them through heavy recurring training plus fully systemized scripts so output doesn't depend on their prior experience.
variants.what_they_do: Hires Filipino chatters at ~$2-3/hour via OnlineJobs.ph, distrusts 'experienced' chatters who spam PPVs, and trains them personally 4-5 times a week (~20-30 min) on his own systems so every chatter sounds identical and 'can't mess up'.

variants.claim: Once initial revenue is generated, the first reinvestment should be hiring chatters on a flat rate, small commission, or hybrid comp structure to free up operator time for scaling.
variants.what_they_do: Describes a reinvestment-first scaling loop: operator chats manually → earns first revenue → hires chatters (flat fee or commission or both) → reclaims time → signs more clients → hires more chatters → repeats. The comp structure is flexible (flat/commission/hybrid) rather than a fixed model.

sources.quote: Go to online jobs.ph. Get yourself a Filipino chatter. Two $3 per hour
teaching your chatters at least three times per week for 30 minutes a day minimum
you can then reinvest it and you can hire what are called chatters. These are people who handle all of the DMs for you, either for a flat rate or a small commission or a mixture of both.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no in-house chatters yet (§8) and no staffing/training model documented (§10 THIN); this informs the future in-house chat team's hiring and training design.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Plan Palm's in-house chat team around systemized scripts + intensive recurring training rather than relying on hires' prior experience.

consensus_risers.topic: Onboarding sequence — OF page setup before social access
consensus_risers.title: Structured 7-day onboarding: OF page + vault first (days 1–4), social access + content plan second (days 5–7)

variants.claim: A sequenced onboarding where the OF page is optimized and chatting infrastructure is ready before social accounts are touched reduces errors and accelerates time-to-revenue.
variants.what_they_do: Days 1–2: get OF login and Inflow access, optimize vault and OF page. Days 3–4: bio, subscription price, page optimization. Days 5–7: gain IG/TikTok/Twitter access, set up content distribution, scripts, chatting setup, and build content plan with creator. Content plan uses the 3-competitor research process (top videos → style selection → post → teach creator what to film and why).

variants.claim: A repeatable onboarding sequence — contract → account access → OF account audit → marketing setup → expectations meeting — prevents early relationship failures.
