variants.what_they_do: After contract signing (via PandaDoc or DocuSign), agency logs into creator's OF account (coordinating 2FA in real time), audits and organizes the subscriber list by spend tier, organizes the content vault, sets up missing social platforms (IG, TikTok, Twitter), sends a content schedule, and holds an expectations-setting conversation covering response windows, platform norms, and month-1/2/3 milestones.

sources.quote: Log in to the only fans in the inflow account. Optimize the vault, optimize the OF page. Then on days like seven, I want you to get into the Instagram, Tik Tok, and Twitter accounts
content strategy and create a content plan with her. So on that, I want you to do niche competitor research
first step is to of course do the onboarding right so the onboarding entails a couple of different things

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's onboarding form captures credentials and contract (§6) but there is no documented day-by-day onboarding sequence or ordering logic. The OF vault/PPV system is thin (§2) and no OF page optimization checklist exists. The social setup is mature for AI accounts (§4) but not formalized for real creators.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Build a real-creator onboarding SOP mirroring the AI warmup cadence structure (§4): Days 1–4 = OF access + page/vault optimization checklist; Days 5–7 = social access + content plan creation. Add this as a checklist workflow in Airtable under the Creators table, triggered on creator status change to 'onboarding.'

consensus_risers.title: Build DM scripts empirically: test freestyle lines, promote high-performing ones into the canonical script

variants.claim: The most effective chat scripts are built bottom-up from live testing — chatters freestyle, track what gets positive responses and high PPV buy rates, and promote those lines/setups into the official script iteratively.
variants.what_they_do: The agency does not hand chatters a finished script to copy-paste. They provide the structural framework (rapport → passive tease → yes train → PPV ladder → gratification close) and then chatters freestyle within it. When a specific line or PPV setup consistently produces positive fan responses or high buy rates, it gets promoted into the agency's canonical script. The script is therefore a living document refined by actual conversion data.

variants.claim: Running three concurrent script variants with measured unlock rates — rather than just deploying a new script agency-wide — reveals the specific element driving performance and prevents agencies from unknowingly using inferior scripts.
variants.what_they_do: When a new chatting script is introduced, the agency assigns variant A, B, and C to different chatters or conversation batches, tracks PPV unlock rate per variant, diagnoses why the winning variant beat the others (e.g., opening line framing), and only then canonises the best approach across all chatters.

sources.quote: If we see that a certain sentence has positive feedback from most subscribers, we will use it in the script. If a PPV has a high buy rate with a certain PPV setup, we will use it in the script
how we create scripts in our agency is we get on the page, we start chatting and see what works
test every single one of them. Okay, this script got a 50% unlock rate. The other variation is 30%, this is 55. Okay, why?

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's chatting is outsourced (§8) and no internal script system exists. For the AI chatting roadmap project specifically, this empirical methodology is directly applicable — Palm could A/B test AI chat lines against fan response data and auto-promote winning variants. This also aligns with Palm's existing data-driven content ideation approach (§1).
palm_comparison.recommendation: When building the in-house chat team and AI chatting system, implement a structured script iteration process: (1) define the structural framework as non-negotiable, (2) allow freestyle within phases, (3) require chatters to flag high-performing lines with a simple tagging system (Airtable or Telegram), (4) conduct weekly script review to promote winners. For AI chatting, this maps to prompt versioning with conversion tracking — build that feedback loop into the architecture from day one.

consensus_risers.title: Google Drive onboarding folder: info sheet + logins + pre-made content scripts

variants.claim: A structured onboarding folder with three components — model info sheet, platform logins, and pre-made PPV content scripts — prevents miscommunication and enables the chatting team to start immediately.
variants.what_they_do: On signing, the agency shares a Google Drive folder. The model info sheet captures personal details (favorite color, food, pet names, etc.) that chatters need to maintain consistent persona in DMs. Logins for all managed platforms are collected at once. Pre-made scripts (photo/video sequences already shot) are also uploaded so chatters can sell PPV from day one. Expectations are set in writing: communication only via WhatsApp group, Monday–Friday 9–5 local time.

variants.claim: If the agency's local machine were destroyed, the business should still be able to operate — so every folder and file that is critical to operations is mirrored on Google Drive in the exact same structure.
variants.what_they_do: The agency maintains a Google Drive that mirrors the local computer: a top-level 'Raw' folder per creator containing sub-folders for raw content, photo grid, invoices, promotional assets, OF stories, and the services agreement (contract). The Raw folder is shared directly with the model (right-click → share access → pink highlight convention so the manager knows it is externally shared) so models can self-upload content.

sources.quote: within our Google Drive folder, we have three things. the model information sheets... the login... and then last but not least is the scripts
if my laptop dropped or fell in a pool or caught on fire or lightning hit it or a car ran it over could the business still operate if the answer is no then I back everything up to the Google Drive
the Royal folder pink that means that this folder has been shared with the model

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's onboarding collects name, email, commission %, state, contract, and voice memo via a form wizard (§6), but has no documented model info sheet for chatters, no platform-login collection protocol, and no pre-made content script intake. Communication boundary-setting is not formalized.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add three items to Palm's onboarding wizard or a linked Google Drive template: (1) a model info sheet with personal details for the chatting team, (2) a platform-login collection step, (3) a pre-made content upload prompt. Also add a written communication expectations section (channel, hours) to the onboarding flow to reduce creator churn friction.

consensus_risers.topic: Fan connection before subscription as LTV driver
consensus_risers.title: Build parasocial fan connection via social persona BEFORE the OF subscribe to raise LTV

variants.claim: Fans who feel connected to a creator's persona — trusting her, finding her funny, being attracted to her personality — before they subscribe spend more and stay longer, measurably raising LTV 20–30% vs. content-first pages.
variants.what_they_do: Griffin's agency builds a distinct social persona for each creator so fans are 'invested before they even spend money.' The persona is expressed across IG/TikTok (top-of-funnel), and the brand promise is consistent with what fans find on OF. This is tracked: they observe LTV and CVR lift of 20–30% after implementing persona-first strategy.

variants.claim: Top agencies do not sell explicit content — they sell synthetic attachment and parasocial engineering, and subscriber drop-off is a retention loop problem, not a traffic problem.
variants.what_they_do: Elite agencies reframe the product as emotional/parasocial dependency rather than explicit content. They diagnose churn as a failure of the retention loop, not insufficient traffic, and build systems around keeping subscribers emotionally tethered for 6+ months.

sources.quote: if you can create that connection before the only fans like they're invested already before they even spend money that's what creates retention
I've seen my LTV go up like 20 30% along with CVR along with all these other metrics
The top agencies do not sell explicit content. They sell synthetic attachment and parasocial engineering.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's social pipeline (§1) is strong on viral content distribution but the baseline doc does not document a persona-consistency check between social identity and OF page experience. Fan retention strategy (§9) tracks whales and going-cold signals but no written retention playbook exists. The persona-to-LTV linkage is not captured.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Formalize a 'brand continuity' checkpoint: after the Creator Identity step (see content finding above), ensure the OF bio, welcome message, and pinned PPV messaging reflect the same persona defined in social. Document this in the onboarding SOP (§6). For §9 fan retention, add persona-fit as a variable when analyzing whale and churn data.

consensus_risers.title: Send a 'gratification video' at session end to convert spent fans into loyal returning subscribers

variants.claim: Sending a warm, smiling 'thank you' video after a fan's spending session — when he is emotionally most vulnerable — builds the relationship bond that drives repeat monetization rather than churn.
variants.what_they_do: When a fan signals spending fatigue, the chatter stops pushing PPVs entirely. Instead, the model sends a pre-filmed 'gratification video': short clip of her smiling, saying she had a great time, expressing genuine-seeming warmth. The chatter then attempts to extend the chat slightly rather than letting the fan leave immediately. The logic: the fan is emotionally open at this moment; a positive close imprints a good memory that makes him return for the full PPV ladder again next session.

variants.claim: Abruptly ending a chat session after a subscriber has spent money makes them feel used and dramatically reduces the chance they return or spend again.
variants.what_they_do: Chatters are explicitly instructed to close every sexting/monetization session with a proper conversational wrap-up — warm sign-off, leaving the subscriber feeling positive and wanting to return. Logging off mid-session is a fireable-level error in the VA training.

sources.quote: this is just a simple video of the model saying thank you and that she had a really good time to the camera being happy smiling. So you make the fan feel good. This is where they're most vulnerable to relationship building
you repeat this process until the fan can't spend anymore. When the fan can't spend anymore, you don't want to force any more sales because this will just ruin the whole relationship

always wrap up the conversation sexting session before logging off this is to leave the subscriber feeling good and wanting to come back for more next time otherwise if you abruptly stop replying the subscriber has spent hundreds of dollars on content they're going to feel used

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has partial fan retention infrastructure (whale hunting, Fan Tracker — §9) but no documented post-session strategy or churn prevention tactic at the individual fan level. This is a concrete retention mechanic that complements the existing Fan Tracker CRM.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add 'gratification close' as a required final step in the in-house chat SOP. Pre-film one gratification video per creator per content set. In the Fan Tracker (§9), add a 'last gratification sent' timestamp field to track recency and ensure it's deployed consistently. Consider this the retention equivalent of a post-purchase email — systematize it the same way.

consensus_risers.title: Use Reddit profile bio as the single OF conversion point; layer IG or Twitter as intermediate funnel where direct OF links are banned

variants.claim: 99% of NSFW subreddits prohibit OF links in posts but allow them in the Reddit profile bio, making profile visits the core conversion mechanic; where even bio links are banned, route traffic through Twitter or Instagram as an intermediate step.
variants.what_they_do: Standard setup: OF link in Reddit profile bio only (not in post body or comments). Post content drives profile clicks; profile bio converts to OF. Where subreddits ban OF bio links (e.g. after saturation): place IG or Twitter link in bio instead, grow those accounts as a secondary funnel, then convert to OF from there. This also unlocks access to additional subreddits that block OF links but permit social links. Reddit DMs used sparingly as a tertiary funnel (limited by Reddit ban risk on high-volume DM sending).

variants.claim: A carefully structured Reddit profile — OF link only in the link field (not posts), a niche-specific one-line bio with a downward arrow, and two pinned model-photo posts with curiosity-driving captions — maximizes profile-to-subscription conversion.
variants.what_they_do: Profile banner: tasteful model preview. Bio: one line, niche-matched, arrow pointing down to links. OF link lives in the Reddit profile link section only — never in the post body. Two pinned posts: model photos with captions crafted to create curiosity, not spammy copy. Link naming convention is optimized for click-through.

sources.quote: basically 99% of the subreddits allows you to post it on your profile on your Reddit profile in the bio. That's it.
instead of getting all these fans and getting to getting them to the only fans straight away… you could also say, okay, I want to funnel them to, for example, an Instagram account
the actual only fence link is in the link section, not the posts

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no Reddit funnel documented (§5 GAP). Palm does run IG accounts and has Publer scheduling (§5), so the IG intermediate-funnel variant aligns with existing infrastructure. The Twitter/X layer is absent from Palm's current stack.
palm_comparison.recommendation: When testing Reddit, default to OF link in bio as the primary CTA. Before launching a creator on Reddit, audit which target subreddits permit OF bio links vs. only social links, and set up the appropriate intermediate account (IG preferred, as Palm already operates IG). Track Reddit → IG → OF conversion separately from direct Reddit → OF to measure intermediate-funnel efficiency.

consensus_risers.title: Pay X Promo Pages $50–$150 per Post to Inject 500–2K Followers Fast

variants.claim: Third-party X 'promotional pages' (50K–500K followers) that post teaser/clickbait content about a creator and then get a staged reply from the creator's account are a highly cost-effective paid acquisition channel on X.
variants.what_they_do: Find accounts (50K+ followers) that post blurry/frame teaser content with clickbait captions like 'Holy shit I can't believe she did this on camera — thread.' Pay them $50–$150 per post. The creator's burner or main account then 'discovers' the post and replies with the real content plus an OF CTA (e.g., 'OMG that's me — I made it free on my OF for the next 17 people'). Identifies promo pages by: always mention 'thread' in caption, post multiple different models, 50K+ followers. Frequency: run a promo every 2–4 weeks per account that is stagnant. One promo post can yield 500–2,000 new followers per day.

variants.claim: Paid placements on X meme pages are profitable traffic sources when the post style and commentary are matched to the audience.
variants.what_they_do: Identify meme pages on X with relevant niche audiences, pay for a sponsored post, and use specific post styles and commentary framing (not disclosed) to maximize click-through to the creator profile. Used selectively — not the primary method — but described as reliably profitable when executed correctly.

sources.quote: These are accounts with absolutely massive following, 50 to 500,000 followers that post teaser content for your models.
I want you to DM them saying, hey, I'm looking to promote my account. What are your rates?
paid promos. We don't do that much, but when we do, they profit. You just find meme pages, pay for a post, funnel people over

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no X strategy at all (§5 GAP). Palm does use paid promotion for IG (implied in pipeline), but no equivalent X promo-page tactic exists. This is a new channel + tactic combination.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Source 3–5 verified X promo pages in each creator's niche. Negotiate a test post at $50–$100. Pre-script the creator's 'reply' post with OF CTA and urgency hook. Track follower gain and OF tracking link conversions per promo. Build a promo-page vendor list in Airtable (name, follower count, niche, rate, past results).

consensus_risers.topic: X content rules — tease vs. reveal
consensus_risers.title: No Full Nudity on X — Use Tease-Only Content to Pull Higher-Spending Fans

variants.claim: Posting full nudity on X attracts low-spending free-content seekers; tease-only premium content attracts higher-demographic buyers who will pay on OF.
variants.what_they_do: Main account posts only professional photos, good angles, clean aesthetic — no full nudity. Full explicit content is reserved exclusively for the OF vault. The X page functions purely as top-of-funnel tease. No OF link on burner accounts — only on the main. Strategy is: build personality + tease body, never reveal. Explicit quote: 'Do tease, but don't reveal. People follow people, not just bodies.'

variants.claim: Directly referencing OnlyFans on social media platforms (IG, TikTok) risks shadowbans or account termination, so all CTAs must be indirect and discreet.
variants.what_they_do: Instructs operators to keep the social-to-OF funnel link-based but never platform-name the destination in captions, bios phrasing, or spoken content. No five-step click chains either — the funnel is: social content → link → OF page, with the OF brand name omitted from the social layer.

sources.quote: Do not post full nudity. Save that for OF. Do tease, but don't reveal.
If you post childish content or you post full nude content, you will pull lower spending fans.
do not mention only fans directly on social media because you are going to get banned. Shadowbanned or worse, right? Just don't do it.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's OF vault content is a documented GAP (§2 — thin). This finding reinforces a content tiering principle: social = tease, OF = explicit. Palm's social pipeline (§1) produces top-of-funnel content but has no explicit rule documented about nudity levels per platform. This is a rule Palm should codify.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add a content-tier rule to Palm's SOP: X/IG/TikTok = tease only (no nudity, professional quality). OF vault = exclusive explicit. Document this as a creator briefing item in onboarding and in the editor task spec so editors never post full nudity to social channels.

consensus_risers.title: Threads 30-slave-account follow-bot: 1,200 targeted leads/day via viral-post follower targeting

variants.claim: Running 30+ Threads slave accounts that each follow 40–50 recent likers of viral similar posts generates ~1,200 targeted daily leads who see the main account funnel.
variants.what_they_do: One main Threads account posts daily content with Fanvue link. 30–50 slave accounts each have bio text 'I'm more active here' tagging the main. A custom Chrome extension bot finds recently viral posts in the niche, filters likes by recency, and auto-follows those users (40–50 per slave per session). Notified users see a profile → visit main → subscribe. AdsPower browser manages all slave accounts. The bot is distributed via private Discord.

variants.claim: A single creator can be scaled across up to 15–20 Threads accounts with near-linear compounding returns if content and operational capacity allow.
variants.what_they_do: TDM runs multiple Threads accounts per creator (up to 15–20), treating content volume and staff/hardware as the only real constraints. They use a phone farm for IG as the mechanism for managing Threads accounts and explicitly avoid automation tools (schedulers/outlers) due to Meta's sensitivity, preferring manual or native posting.

sources.quote: You need 30 50 of these. They just have to post and in the bar you can say I'm more active here and tag your main account
30 slave accounts, multiply by 40 follows, 1,200 targeted leads a day, you will get subscribers

if you've got enough content and the content performs and you see return on investment, you could run up to 15 20 accounts per creator. The bottleneck here is often times the manpower to actually run that and the hardware

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented Threads strategy or follower-growth automation (§5 🔲 GAP). Palm does use Publer for scheduling but not engagement automation. This level of account farming on Threads is outside Palm's current documented ops.
palm_comparison.recommendation: For the AI persona scaling line (§3), evaluate the Threads slave-account follow strategy as a traffic lever. The math (1,200 leads/day) is compelling if sustainable. Before adopting: assess Threads TOS risk (similar to IG follow-loop risks) and whether AdsPower fits alongside existing GrapheneOS/hardware isolation setup (§4). Start with a small 5-account pilot before scaling to 30+.

consensus_risers.title: Snapchat + read-only Telegram channel as a ban-resilient backup funnel

variants.claim: Operators add low-effort Snapchat reach and an owned Telegram broadcast channel to nurture leads and survive social bans, since owned audiences persist when accounts die.
variants.what_they_do: ~15 min/day Snapchat: public profile, repurposed 9:16 content, 2-3 stories/day, Telegram CTA only once/week at peak time, no DMs/links to avoid bans; funnels into a read-only Telegram broadcast channel (pinned Fanvue link, niche real-life teasing, reaction-bait) that both converts and acts as a 'safe haven' to relaunch socials after a ban.

variants.claim: Snapchat is an under-exploited OFM channel that simultaneously drives OF subs, cross-promotes other platforms, and pays direct creator monetization.
variants.what_they_do: Sets up a real-looking account, posts ~5 spotlights/day plus 30-100 clickbait story snaps/day (repurposed via repurpose.io or a VA), CTAs only once a week to avoid disabling, and uses paid promoted-snap boosts; cites a creator earning as much from Snapchat as from Instagram.

sources.quote: Over here we have the Telegram funnel
builds a safe haven for leads on your Telegram if a social ban occurs
boost your initial following

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's distribution is Publer + Telegram-to-poster (§5) with no documented owned-audience/backup-channel or Snapchat strategy; traffic strategy is a gap (§5).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Pilot an owned Telegram (or email) audience as a ban-resilient backup channel for real creators, plus Snapchat as a cheap secondary funnel; reduces single-platform dependency risk.

consensus_risers.topic: Social content strategy — filler vs. vertical content split
consensus_risers.title: Two-content-type IG framework: 'filler' (lifestyle) keeps algorithm happy; 'vertical' (viral-engineered) drives new traffic

variants.claim: Posting 2–3 Reels per day split between algorithm-maintenance lifestyle content and virality-engineered vertical content is the primary Instagram growth method for driving OF subscriptions.
variants.what_they_do: 2–3 Reels/day. Filler content = lifestyle/personal content to keep the account active and algorithm-fed. Vertical content = explicitly engineered for virality — Snapchat-style verticals, talking-head videos, mirror videos. Both types use 'link in bio' and pinned comments directing to OF. Competitor analysis is central: identify top-performing competitor videos, isolate the 3 best, test posting similar styles, then use performance data to teach the creator what to film and why it works (lighting, facial expression, movement).

variants.claim: Locking a creator into specific content verticals aligned with her strengths makes results predictable and gives the agency a repeatable creative direction model.
variants.what_they_do: For each creator, Ellis identifies her natural strengths and interests, maps them to 2–3 content verticals (e.g. cooking niche with cooking outfits), produces content across those verticals for ~6 weeks, analyses performance data, extracts the viral ingredients, and scales what works. The creator's brief is narrow enough that ideas are abundant and filming is sustainable.

sources.quote: Filler content is just going to be her lifestyle. This keeps the algorithm happy… Vertical content is what brings new people into your page
Find three competitors. Make a list of the top performing videos. Take the three most performing videos. Put that in a style selection process
once you've found the content verticals, it's about trying to turn the creator into the best creator within that space — results then become predictable

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has a mature 8-step inspo-driven pipeline that uses viral data to decide what creators film (§1), which directly parallels the competitor analysis piece here. However, Palm's posting cadence (frequency, time-of-day) is not codified (§1 GAP). The filler vs. vertical content taxonomy is not explicitly documented in Palm's system.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Formally adopt the filler/vertical taxonomy inside Palm's content pipeline. Tag each Asset in Airtable as 'filler' or 'vertical/traffic-driver' so editors and schedulers can enforce the 2–3/day cadence with the right mix. This codifies the cadence GAP noted in §1.

consensus_risers.topic: Platform-native content formats for AI model social
consensus_risers.title: Format-match by platform: long-caption storytelling on IG, slideshows on TikTok, text posts on Threads

variants.claim: Each platform rewards a specific content format; matching format to platform algorithm dramatically increases reach and watch time for AI model accounts.
variants.what_they_do: Instagram: long-caption 'storytelling' posts where the persona narrates a relatable story (ex-drama etc.) — increases watch time which triggers broader algorithmic push; captions generated via ChatGPT from real OFI model examples. Also 'caption hook videos' with an on-screen text hook. TikTok: slideshow format prioritized because TikTok is actively promoting slides and they go viral more easily; targets lonely-men content angles. Threads: short punchy text posts ('somebody FaceTime me') that feel real and prompt engagement from the target demographic.

variants.claim: Carousel posts move the needle most on Threads, but text/comment posts are needed as cheap filler to hit daily volume targets.
variants.what_they_do: TDM designates carousel posts as their primary growth format ('goats') and text/comment posts as filler. Of the 5–8 daily posts, at least a couple are carousels; the rest are short text posts. They prioritise content that is shareable and/or controversial as the single biggest lever.

sources.quote: Find some good storytelling post from OFI models on IG and use chat GTP to make your own
when it comes to Tik Tok you're going to want to do more slides because Tik Tok really favors slides
comment posts as your fillers and then carousel posts as the goats. These are the kind of things that are really going to move the needle with your page

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's content engine (§1) is strong at inspo-scraping and replicating viral Reels but is built around video for IG. The Airtable pipeline does not appear to have a Threads text-post workflow or TikTok slideshow format lane. Content format strategy is not documented per-platform.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Extend Palm's content pipeline (§1) to include platform-specific format lanes: (1) TikTok slideshow template workflow, (2) Threads text-post templates. For real creators, these formats can supplement the existing Reels-first approach. For AI personas, add ChatGPT-assisted long-caption storytelling as a content type in the `Inspiration`/`Assets` pipeline.

consensus_risers.topic: Reddit asset spoofing to prevent cross-account detection
consensus_risers.title: Spoof every reused image via @spoofsybot; use RedGIFs re-upload to spoof videos

variants.claim: Reusing un-spoofed images or videos across Reddit accounts links them and triggers chain bans; spoofing tools strip the metadata/hash that Reddit uses to connect accounts.
variants.what_they_do: For images: run all reused assets through @spoofsybot on Telegram (paid, cheap, offers free trial) before posting. For videos/GIFs: upload fresh to RedGIFs each time — RedGIFs auto-generates a new file fingerprint, functionally spoofing the video. Never repost raw files across accounts.

variants.claim: Creating Reddit accounts on a mobile data plan (never Wi-Fi) is critical to avoiding immediate flagging and bans.
variants.what_they_do: Every account is created fresh on mobile with a cellular data plan. No purchased accounts, no Wi-Fi. Franko tried buying accounts in 2024 and says it did not work. All accounts are self-made from scratch on mobile data.

sources.quote: spoofsybot on Telegram… you can spoof images there and you reuse content. Make sure you always do if you want to ever reuse content, you have to always spoof it
Red GFs technically does it for you because all you have to do in red gifs is just upload the video again and use a different link
we make all our accounts on mobile with a data plan and we never touch Wi-Fi

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has a mature AI-persona account isolation system (§3 — hardware-isolated, separate SIMs, GrapheneOS) but no documented asset-spoofing workflow for Reddit or any platform. This is a direct gap when scaling multi-account Reddit traffic.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add asset-spoofing as a mandatory step in any Reddit posting SOP: integrate @spoofsybot into the content-prep checklist for images and enforce RedGIFs-only video uploads. Document this in the platform-risk runbook alongside existing device-isolation procedures.

consensus_risers.title: Strip AI metadata before posting or reach is throttled

variants.claim: AI-generated media carries hidden metadata that platforms read to tag/throttle content, so removing it before posting materially restores reach.
variants.what_they_do: Removes AI metadata from generated images/videos with a free tool or re-export before posting; reports reach went up ~40% and videos hit explore again after they started doing this.

variants.claim: Platforms now algorithmically detect AI-generated images via invisible watermarks/metadata, so operators must avoid watermarking generators and look human to survive ban waves.
variants.what_they_do: Blames a wave of Instagram bans on Nano Banana Pro images carrying Google SynthID + metadata that Meta detects and flags as fraud/deception; fix is to generate with tools that leave no SynthID/metadata, avoid OF/Fanvue logos and links in bio, list multiple socials to look human, and use lower-profile link providers (Linktree over flagged Link.me).

sources.quote: there's a metadata attached to those files that says AI
Google Nano Banana Pro has, what we discussed earlier, something called a Synth ID
the most common ban reason is fraud and deception

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's AI-persona program is built for compliance (EU AI Act label + watermark, §3), which is the opposite posture. This is a competitor risk tactic Palm should note but weigh against its compliance stance.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Note as a reach lever for non-EU/unlabeled experiments only; do NOT apply to Palm's EU-AI-Act-labeled personas. Flag the compliance tension for leadership.

consensus_risers.topic: AI reel animation — 'secret prompt' technique for virality
consensus_risers.title: Extract the core action from a viral reel as the animation prompt to replicate its hook

variants.claim: The key to making an AI-animated reel go viral is identifying the specific physical action in the original viral reel and using that exact action as the animation prompt, rather than a generic description.
variants.what_they_do: When a viral reel is identified (e.g., 1M views), the operator distills the core action ('girl is doing a backflip') as the animation prompt fed into the AI tool (Kling). They then ask the AI tool to refine and make the prompt 'as realistic as possible.' The first frame of the recreated image is animated with this action prompt.

variants.claim: Sending creators viral content from models who look like them — same hair color, body type, vibe — dramatically increases the likelihood the creator will replicate it well and that the content will resonate with the right audience.
variants.what_they_do: Agency manually finds a model whose look closely matches the new creator (e.g., 'blonde cheerleader type'), identifies that model's currently-viral videos, and sends them as direct filming references. Creator replicates the format/dance/hook on IG and TikTok.

sources.quote: the secret prompt is what the original reel is doing to make it viral… if the girl is doing a backflip the secret prompt is going to be the girl is doing a backflip
ask the deep fake to refine the prompt and make it as much realistic as possible
try and find her ideas for Content that are similar to her looking so if she's a blond kind of cheerleader type model then fantastic find another model who looks like her and is currently going viral

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's §1 pipeline includes AI vision analysis of inspo reels (transcript, visual breakdown, hook, viral score) but the output is a filming idea for a real creator, not an animation prompt for synthetic video. This technique is specifically for AI content production and has no current Palm equivalent. The underlying logic — isolate the hook action, not just the aesthetic — is relevant to Palm's brief-writing for real creators too.
palm_comparison.recommendation: For the AI persona build-toward (§3/§0): incorporate 'action extraction prompt' as a named step in the synthetic reel SOP — after screenshotting inspo, explicitly ask GPT 'what is the specific physical action happening in this reel?' and use that answer as the primary animation prompt. For real creators: consider adding 'primary hook action' as a structured field in the inspo analysis output (§1, step 3) to sharpen filming briefs.

consensus_risers.title: Girlfriend-experience parasocial selling, not PPV spam

variants.claim: The girlfriend experience (parasocial connection, scripted live-feeling sells, no PPV spam) drives the highest and most durable chatting revenue.
variants.what_they_do: Optimizes the OF wall to feel like 'Snapchatting,' uses 7-10 stage scripts with verification images, never initiates sexual content (lets the fan initiate), handles broke fans with ego-protecting lines, and forbids PPV spamming across the whole team.

variants.claim: Top agencies do not sell explicit content — they sell synthetic attachment and parasocial engineering, and subscriber drop-off is a retention loop problem, not a traffic problem.
variants.what_they_do: Elite agencies reframe the product as emotional/parasocial dependency rather than explicit content. They diagnose churn as a failure of the retention loop, not insufficient traffic, and build systems around keeping subscribers emotionally tethered for 6+ months.

sources.quote: the GF experience, the only thing that I try to run in OFM
The top agencies do not sell explicit content. They sell synthetic attachment and parasocial engineering.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Chatting is OUTSOURCED at Palm with no in-house SOP (§8). This is direct build-toward intelligence for Palm's planned in-house and AI chat teams.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Capture the GFE framework (wall optimization, staged scripts, verification, no-spam rule) as the seed SOP for Palm's future in-house/AI chatting.

consensus_risers.title: Build so the founder can disappear

variants.claim: Heavy supervision layers and clear workflows let the founder step away while the business keeps running, which is the real test of a scaled agency.
variants.what_they_do: Hive Media (300 employees, 60 models) layers supervisors into category managers into shift/market managers per department, and the founder says if he disappeared for six months the company would still run profitably.

variants.claim: The only reliable way to test whether a $100k/month agency is truly scalable is for the founder to take a full week off and observe whether revenue, creator satisfaction, and employee efficiency hold without them.
variants.what_they_do: Liam prescribes a literal one-week founder absence as a diagnostic: if GMV and creator satisfaction hold or improve, the business is scalable; if revenue drops or creator issues go unhandled, the business is still founder-dependent and specific SOPs are missing.

sources.quote: if I disappear for 6 months right now, will my company grow? Maybe not exponentially, but it will run
take a week off and just observe what happens

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has a thin org with many decisions through the founder and no org chart or scaling plan (§10 THIN). Direct gap.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Build a supervision layer (team leads / managers) per Palm function with explicit workflows so the founder can step out without the operation stalling.

consensus_risers.title: Identify and solve one operational bottleneck at a time rather than fixing everything at once

variants.claim: Agencies scale most effectively by diagnosing the single largest bottleneck — social coverage, chatter quality, chatter hours — and solving only that before moving to the next.
variants.what_they_do: Dylan walks through a sequential bottleneck-resolution framework: (1) Could more social accounts drive more revenue? Yes → outsource social. (2) Could 24/7 chatting earn more? Yes → hire chatters. (3) Could better-trained chatters earn more? Yes → build chatter training. Each step is solved fully before the next is attempted. He explicitly warns against trying to solve all problems simultaneously, which leads to overwhelm and paralysis.

variants.claim: The most practical way to identify scaling bottlenecks is to ask 'what would break if our revenue doubled overnight' rather than asking 'how do we grow.'
variants.what_they_do: TDM frames the scaling question as a stress-test thought experiment: list every department and workflow, then identify which ones would snap under 2x load. The output is a prioritized list of systems, hires, and automations to build before growth demands them, not after.

sources.quote: look at where your bottlenecks are in your business and work from there so you know could you make more money with a client if you had more social media accounts running traffic yes okay well how do you do that
what would happen and what would break if revenue doubled tomorrow

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm is actively running multiple parallel initiatives (SMM hub redesign §11, AI chatting project §8, warmup improvements §4, Phase 3 Publer §5). The risk Dylan describes — solving everything at once — is live. Palm does not have a documented prioritization framework for which bottleneck to tackle first.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Adopt a formal bottleneck log in Airtable or the admin portal: a ranked list of operational constraints with a single 'active focus' flag. At each leadership sync, confirm only one bottleneck is in active resolution. This isn't about slowing down — it's about ensuring each fix is fully deployed and measured before the next one starts. Apply this now to sequence the SMM consolidation, chat buildout, and Phase 3 Publer work.

consensus_risers.title: Spreadsheet/Airtable backend at scale

variants.claim: Running account and content operations off spreadsheet-style databases with front-end interfaces is the pragmatic backbone for managing accounts at scale.
variants.what_they_do: Hive Media stores 10,000+ social accounts (proxy, 2FA, status, creation date) in Excel/Sheets as the database with front-end interfaces on top, updated automatically by bots, and explicitly says Airtable is just a prettier version of the same database approach.

variants.claim: A scaled agency runs on connected central databases (accounts, team, content, finance) so operational detail lives in systems, not the owner's head.
variants.what_they_do: Runs six linked Notion systems (model account manager with logins/warmup SOPs, VA/team tracker with pay+KPIs, content HQ, finance tracker, iPhone tracker, AI to-do list) that interlink so the owner only reviews and decides.

sources.quote: the only thing better than a spreadsheet might be a slightly prettier spreadsheet in something like Air Table
the model account manager

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm already runs on Airtable (HQ + Content Ops bases) with a Next.js admin front end (§0), so Palm is aligned with this best practice. Validation, not a gap.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Keep the Airtable-as-database plus admin-UI pattern; ensure account-status fields (proxy, ban status, creation date) are tracked and auto-updated as rigorously as Hive does.

consensus_risers.title: Target mid-tier creators (10K followers, ~$1-3K/mo) not top earners

variants.claim: Beginner agencies should target creators with ~10-15K followers already making $1-3K/month rather than chasing large verified accounts who won't respond.
variants.what_they_do: Explicitly filter outreach to creators in the 10K-15K follower range earning roughly $1-3K/month on OF, on the basis that they are more open to support, more reachable via DM, and more likely to convert to a managed relationship.

variants.claim: Agencies should segment potential creator clients into four revenue bands and target the tier where their service delivers the highest ROI.
variants.what_they_do: Dylan segments OF creators into four bands: $0–10K/mo, $10–50K/mo, $100–500K/mo, and $500K+/mo. New/smaller agencies are directed to target the $0–10K band because those creators get the biggest ROI from management services. He sends clients a segmentation sheet and has them identify which band to focus on exclusively, so outreach and pitching is concentrated rather than scattered.

sources.quote: targeting more beginner friendly creators as well. Also, someone that's kind of starting out, 10,000 15,000 followers, ones that are also making around 10K a month or so

we split there up into four market segments right only fans models making 500k a month only fans models making between 100 500k a month only fans models making 10 to 50K a month and only fans models anything from 0 to 10K

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's acquisition is Josh-DM-led with no documented targeting criteria (§6 GAP). No internal baseline exists for follower count, current earnings, or engagement thresholds that qualify a creator for outreach.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Codify a minimum viable creator profile for outreach: e.g., 10K-20K IG followers, $1-3K/mo existing OF revenue, active posting cadence. Add these as qualification fields to the creator onboarding form or a pre-onboarding screening step in Airtable.

consensus_risers.title: Target creators who are plateaued, overwhelmed, or burned by a bad agency

variants.claim: The highest-converting creator prospects are those who are stuck at a plateau, overwhelmed by workload, or have had a bad agency experience — not cold beginners.
variants.what_they_do: Agency explicitly looks for creators who: (a) plateaued after initial growth, (b) are overwhelmed managing content + chatting alone, (c) had a bad prior agency and are open to a better partner. Uses these pain points as the outreach hook.

variants.claim: Low-tier models produce low-tier results regardless of agency skill, so raising the minimum quality bar of creators signed is a prerequisite to scaling past $50K/month.
variants.what_they_do: Ellis explicitly rejects creators with low work ethic regardless of other factors. He qualifies inbound leads using story-driven case-study content (e.g. 'how we took a creator from 0 to 40K') that pre-filters serious creators, rather than vanity result screenshots. The content itself does the qualification work before any call.

sources.quote: you're looking for models that are a bit overwhelmed, they're a bit stuck, their accounts are being plateaued, maybe they've had a bad agency before
if you keep acquiring low tier models, you're going to get low tier results because you got low tier work ethic and it's not going to scale

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented ICP (ideal creator profile) or qualification criteria for outbound (§6). Josh-led DM outreach is ad hoc. Targeting burned/plateaued creators is a concrete, testable filter Palm could apply immediately.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Define Palm's creator ICP in writing: earning $1K–$10K/mo, plateaued 60+ days, active IG but not growing, or recently left an agency. Use these as DM targeting filters on IG/TikTok. Add a qualification question to the onboarding form: 'Have you worked with an agency before? What happened?'

consensus_risers.topic: Creator vetting criteria and red-flag screening
consensus_risers.title: Screen creators for effort-mindset before signing; avoid purely passive-income-seekers

variants.claim: Agencies should qualify creators on mindset — specifically filtering out those who want purely passive income — because they disengage during the experimentation phase and waste agency resources.
variants.what_they_do: The speakers describe two acquisition filters: (1) prefer creators who have already tried OF themselves and seen some traction, because they understand the work involved and appreciate agency value more; (2) screen out creators who articulate 'I just want to make money passively' as their primary motivation, since they won't fulfill content obligations and the agency's marketing investment becomes a gamble. They also describe a 7-step creator vetting process for creators evaluating agencies (social proof → documented process → call → ask deliverable questions → watch for red flags like guaranteed numbers or pressure to sign quickly), which implicitly describes what a credible agency's pitch should contain.

variants.claim: How a model responds during onboarding — responsiveness, receptiveness to feedback, implementation of early requests — is a leading indicator of her long-term revenue potential and whether the agency should continue investing in her.
variants.what_they_do: They observe model behavior during the onboarding phase as a quality signal: slow responses, failure to implement feedback, or resistance to criticism are treated as early warnings that the creator will not reach top-tier earnings. They acknowledge that new agencies must work with whoever they can get but advise building judgment over time to screen for quality clients.

sources.quote: me personally when it comes to new creators I definitely would say I'm kind of looking at the mindset that they're in before coming into this like if they're just in this I just want to make money passively then forget it it's not going to work
if they're flaky or they don't respond very good or they take forever to respond or they don't Implement criticism you most likely don't have a very high quality client

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's acquisition is thin (§6) with no documented qualification criteria or creator scoring beyond the onboarding form fields. There is no mindset screen or preference framework for experienced-vs-new creators. The onboarding wizard captures logistics (name, commission, contract) but not fit signals. Palm's 75-convo market research was acquisition discovery, not an ongoing qualification system.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add a creator qualification layer before the onboarding form is sent: a 5-10 minute discovery call or async screening form with 3-4 questions targeting: (1) how long have they been on OF and what's their current monthly range, (2) what are they willing to commit to weekly in content production, (3) what's their primary goal (scale income vs. passive). Define a simple pass/fail criteria. Prioritize creators already making $1-5k/month who understand the platform — the speakers call this the ideal profile for fast results and agency appreciation.

consensus_risers.title: New agencies should master one creator before signing multiple

variants.claim: Beginners who try to sign many clients simultaneously disappoint everyone and burn out; mastering operations on one client first produces better outcomes.
variants.what_they_do: Dylan explicitly advises new agency operators to sign a single creator, test all marketing and chatting strategies on that one account, and only expand once they know what works. He argues that without in-house chatters (which beginners can't afford) and without proven marketing, having five-plus clients is unmanageable and leads to poor results across the board.

variants.claim: New agencies should aim to sign two clients simultaneously: one as a hedge if the other underperforms, but no more than two to avoid operational overwhelm before systems are proven.
variants.what_they_do: Prescribes a deliberate two-client start: if one relationship fails the other provides continuity, while capping at two prevents the operator from over-extending before they have SOPs, chatters, or reliable traffic in place.

sources.quote: focus on one client to begin with because a lot of beginners will try and just like scatter gun and try and get as many clients as they can thinking that that is going to solve their problems
you should ideally aim to sign two clients from the get-go. If you just have one, you can stay with one, of course, but in most cases, one of them might not work out

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's acquisition approach is Josh-DM-led without a documented onboarding capacity limit (§6). There is no written rule about how many creators Palm takes on relative to operational readiness (chatting outsourced §8, thin content vault §2). This principle is implicitly understood but not codified as a scaling gate.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Define and document Palm's current operational capacity ceiling per creator tier — e.g., how many creators can be served well given current editor headcount, outsourced chat team bandwidth, and social pipeline throughput. Use this as a hard gate in the acquisition process so new signings only happen when capacity exists, preventing the quality dilution Dylan describes.

consensus_risers.topic: IG ban survivability via satellite account bench
consensus_risers.title: Pre-warm satellite IG accounts as instant replacement bench when main account is banned

variants.claim: Maintaining a pool of continuously warmed satellite accounts means a creator can immediately resume posting on a new account the day their main is banned, with zero cold-start penalty.
variants.what_they_do: Because satellite accounts are already being warmed daily (feed scrolling, posting, engagement), they have account age and trust score built up. When the creator's primary IG account is banned, the agency hands over one or more of these pre-warmed accounts for the creator to continue posting from immediately, avoiding the traffic gap that causes creator churn.

variants.claim: Assuming a baseline account-ban burn rate and pre-provisioning reserve accounts is required to run social acquisition at scale without interruption.
variants.what_they_do: Hive Media issues 200 accounts per platform per model (about 100 active immediately, 100 in reserve), runs a daily script using public profile views to auto-detect bans, and accepts that even fully organic operations always carry some burn.

sources.quote: if the creators' main accounts do get banned, we have a lot of other accounts which we can just give them, and they can start posting on them, because we've been growing these accounts anyway
100 that become active immediately when they start then 100 for reserve... there's always the risk of having some bans

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's AI-persona warmup system (§3/§4) achieves exactly this for synthetic creators — a 90-day warmup produces a ready-to-use account. The same logic is not applied to real-creator backup accounts (§4 🔲 GAP). This is a direct extension of Palm's existing capability to the real-creator line.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Maintain a small bench (3–5) of pre-warmed IG accounts per real creator. Palm's existing warmup operator UI and Publer scheduling could drive this with minimal incremental tooling. Define a handoff SOP: when main account ban is confirmed, assign bench account to creator within 24 hours. This directly reduces model churn from traffic loss (a live pain point per §6 — model retention).

new_evidence.title: Warm fans with a slightly discounted first PPV, then escalate price on every subsequent send

variants.claim: The first PPV sale is a warm-up purchase at a slight discount; once a fan buys it, every future PPV should be priced at the floor or higher, escalating as the conversation progresses.
variants.what_they_do: New subscribers receive an automated script that pitches a first video at $34.99 (slightly below the $39.99 floor) to reduce friction and get a first purchase. After that purchase the fan is considered 'warmed up' and all future content is priced at $40+ for video and $20+ for images, escalating upward as the sexting session progresses. The escalation is tied to emotional momentum — the longer the conversation runs, the more the fan will pay.

variants.claim: Pricing the first PPV low (roughly $5-$20) exists to get the fan used to spending, not to make money on that sale.

variants.claim: Sending low-ticket PPVs ($15–$20) first gauges a subscriber's willingness to spend and builds purchasing habit before high-ticket ($80–$100+) asks.
variants.what_they_do: Chatter begins by offering content in the $15–$20 range ('dipping a toe in the water'). After multiple purchases at this level, the chatter reads the subscriber's comfort and escalates to high-ticket PPVs ($80, $90, $100+). No high-ticket offer is sent cold to a new subscriber.

variants.claim: The first PPV sale should always be $5 or less to condition the subscriber to buy; subsequent sales increase in price incrementally, and the overall set is structured as 7 separate sales attempts at progressively higher price points.
variants.what_they_do: Chatters are instructed to structure one content 'set' as 7 distinct sales opportunities (individual pics, pic+video combos, etc.) with prices rising at each step. The first sale is always ≤$5 — not because the content is cheap, but to make the buy frictionless and establish a Pavlovian habit: sub sees PPV → sub unlocks. Subs who won't spend $5 are deprioritized or ignored. New chatters are taught to use small price increments (e.g., $5 → $7 → $10) rather than jumping to $25+. Selling too much too soon is explicitly called out as a sub-burning mistake; longevity and lifetime value are the stated goal.

variants.claim: A soft verbal commitment ('yes train') before sending the first PPV — combined with a $15–$25 price point and partial-nudity content — significantly increases buy-through rates.
variants.what_they_do: Before sending any PPV, the chatter runs a 'yes train': a question that sounds like consent ('if I do this, do you promise you'll go easy on me?') but functions as a purchase pre-commitment. When the fan says yes, he feels psychologically obligated to buy the PPV that follows. First PPV is priced $15–$25 (higher if the model is positioned as premium; lower if marketed with explicit free content). Content is partial nudity only (top off). Subsequent PPVs escalate: bottoms off → full nude → solo play. Between each PPV, the chatter rebuilds arousal with free teasers and descriptive text before the next ask. The session ends when the fan signals spending fatigue — at which point no further PPV is pushed.

variants.claim: Starting the PPV ladder at a low price ($15) minimizes the psychological barrier to the first purchase, making it far more likely the subscriber enters the escalation funnel and ultimately spends $300–$500.
variants.what_they_do: The specific ladder cited: $15 → $30 → $60 → $120 → $200 → $200. Entry price is low enough to feel trivial. Each step roughly doubles, creating anchoring where each subsequent price feels proportional to the last rather than shocking. The final $200 items are 'full action' — maximum explicitness justifying maximum price. Total session value: ~$625 at full conversion.

new_evidence.topic: IG content quality control and outsourcing
new_evidence.title: Score-checklist system converts vague creative quality judgments into binary yes/no criteria for delegation

variants.claim: Subjective creative quality assessments (good background, good hook, good energy) can't be delegated until every parameter is broken into yes/no binary questions that produce a numeric score, enabling non-creative employees to do quality control.
variants.what_they_do: For IG reels, the agency builds a checklist where every quality criterion is expressed as a yes/no question: 'Would you want to be in this location right now? Yes/No.' 'Does the location look like she actually lives there? Yes/No.' 'Did you see this location in the last 10 reels? Yes/No.' Each yes/no maps to a score; if the score falls below a threshold the employee must re-evaluate specific items. For training the marketing brain, new hires start as researchers only — sending 10 reference videos per day for 3 weeks — with daily feedback on which were good/bad and why. Only after 7/10 research picks are consistently good do they advance to writing briefs, then to managing a creator. The owner records Loom commentary on 10 reels daily ('1, 5, 7, 9 have good backgrounds; 2, 6, 9 have bad ones — here's why') to build the employee's feedback loop.

variants.claim: The difference between 6K-view and 6M-view reels is an engineered three-part formula: matching aesthetics to creator USP, a scripted look-away→look-in→smile emotional arc, and a deliberate stop-scroll pattern-interrupt moment.
variants.what_they_do: Aesthetics: outfit, location, lighting, framing all matched to the creator's visual USP (e.g., if USP is large chest + black hair, 80% of content angles highlight that — not hidden in hoodies). Emotional curve: reels are scripted with a 3-beat arc — look away (living her life) → look in (she noticed you) → smile (girlfriend moment) — engineered to create GFE in 3 seconds, repeated across the ~46 views a typical fan sees before subscribing. WTF moment: every reel has a deliberate background event (someone walking into a pole, a cat crossing) that breaks autopilot scrolling and triggers rewatches, which signals the algorithm.

variants.what_they_do: Three-layer pre-screen: (1) embed a hidden instruction in the job post requiring a specific phrase to prove they read it; (2) on onlinejobs.ph, prioritize applicants who spent more Application Points (higher AP = higher motivation, lower AP = mass-applying); (3) send all applicants a structured template (name, age, experience, English level, open-ended questions like 'describe your communication style') and filter out anyone who answers lazily or skips fields.

variants.claim: A slow, multi-stage hiring funnel with strict filtering criteria is cheaper than hiring the wrong person, whose true cost in lost time, training, and mistakes runs into tens of thousands of dollars.
variants.what_they_do: Agency runs a dedicated hiring funnel lasting roughly one week: job post → multiple filtering stages → skills/personality tests → pick only the single best-qualified applicant. If no applicant clears the bar, the owner does the job themselves for another 2 weeks and re-runs the funnel rather than settling. Once hired, the employee gets a fully thought-through onboarding, individual KPI tracking, and formal performance reviews.

new_evidence.topic: Multi-account setup — preventing Instagram account linkage
new_evidence.title: Differentiate every signal across repurposing accounts to prevent IG cluster-ban

variants.claim: Instagram actively clusters accounts that share profile pictures, sounds, bios, links, and feed content, then bans them as a group.
variants.what_they_do: Repurposing accounts must have: completely different usernames (not sequential variations), different profile pictures, different feed posts, different bios, and different funneling links (Beacons vs Linktree vs direct, etc.). variants.claim: Posting identical video sequences across all repurposing accounts is the most common way agencies trigger cluster-bans.
variants.what_they_do: If a creator films 3 videos/day, all 3 go to the main organic account, but each repurposing account receives only ONE of the three — a different one per account. This ensures no two reposting accounts share the same Reels grid sequence.

variants.claim: Mixing original and repurposed content on one account cancels out the benefits of organic reach while still incurring the algorithmic penalty of repurposed content.
variants.what_they_do: They operate two structurally separate account tiers: (1) main organic accounts where the creator films fresh content every time — these pull the highest views; (2) repurposing-only accounts staffed by VAs, purely for volume. The two tiers are never merged.

variants.claim: Mixing original and repurposed content on one account cancels out the benefits of organic reach while still incurring the algorithmic penalty of repurposed content.
variants.what_they_do: They operate two structurally separate account tiers: (1) main organic accounts where the creator films fresh content every time — these pull the highest views; (2) repurposing-only accounts staffed by VAs, purely for volume. The two tiers are never merged.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's content pipeline (§1) produces organic, creator-filmed Reels and does not currently have a repurposing workflow. The structural-edit technique is a distinct add-on to the existing pipeline for any scaled reposting layer.
palm_comparison.recommendation: If Palm ever runs multi-account reposting for a single creator, document a mandatory structural-edit checklist (scene trim, mirror, new intro scene + minor color grade) before export. Add this as a step in the existing pipeline for repurposed variants, distinct from the organic primary post.

new_evidence.title: Real creator + AI clone, kept on OnlyFans

variants.claim: Cloning a real signed creator into an AI model and mixing AI with real content lets an agency keep OnlyFans conversion while multiplying volume.
variants.what_they_do: Take 20-30 photos of a real signed creator, train an AI model on her face/body, generate motion-control videos, then post a real+AI mix that all funnels to her real OnlyFans (no AI sold past the wall). One model films 35-40 videos/week; AI fills the other 40-45, enabling 4+ accounts.

variants.claim: Full-AI creators are forced onto less-trusted third-party platforms where conversion drops 30-50% versus OnlyFans, making hybrid (real OF) structurally more profitable.
variants.what_they_do: Argues full AI cannot use OnlyFans (ID verification, no AI content allowed) so they go to Fansly/Fanvue/Fanfix where saved-credit-card trust is absent and conversion tanks; hybrid keeps everything on OnlyFans.

sources.quote: you take a real creator and you generate your own AI profile of that creator
Your conversion rates will literally drop 30 to 50% just by doing that platform rather than Only Fans

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm already runs a recreation pipeline for AI personas funneling to OnlyFans (§3), but does NOT yet clone REAL creators to scale their social volume. This is a direct extension of Palm's existing AI-persona capability onto the real-creator core business (§0/§1).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Pilot cloning one cooperative real creator: train an AI likeness, post a real/AI mix to her existing funnel, keep all OF vault content 100% real. Leverage Palm's existing recreation pipeline.
